Inside a single organization (one company, one campus), the routers cooperate with an **interior gateway protocol (IGP)**. Two beginner-friendly examples:

- **RIP** is the simplest. Each router just asks its direct neighbors, "how far away is each destination?" and picks the path with the fewest hops. Because routers learn only from neighbors (a bit like passing along rumors), RIP is easy to understand but slow and limited; it is mostly of historical interest now.
- **OSPF** is smarter and far more common. Every router shares a description of its own links, so all of them build the **same map** of the network, then each computes the shortest path across that map. Because they share a real map instead of rumors, OSPF reacts faster and scales better.

The two styles have names: RIP is **distance-vector** (learn distances from neighbors), OSPF is **link-state** (everyone builds a full map).

**Recap:** when several routes match, the most specific one wins (longest-prefix match). Static routes are typed by hand; dynamic routing protocols fill tables automatically. Inside one organization, RIP (distance-vector, simple) and OSPF (link-state, map-based) are the classic interior protocols.

items.prompt: A router's table has 0.0.0.0/0, 10.0.0.0/8, and 10.1.2.0/24. A packet is going to 10.1.2.130. Which rule does the router use?
choices: 0.0.0.0/0, because the default route is always tried first
10.0.0.0/8, because it covers the most addresses
10.1.2.0/24, because it is the most specific rule that matches
It uses all three at once and splits the packet
items.explanation: All three ranges contain 10.1.2.130, but the router always chooses the most specific match, the longest prefix. /24 is more specific than /8, and far more specific than the /0 default. Covering the most addresses is exactly why the /8 is LESS specific, so it loses. A packet is never split across rules.

items.prompt: What does 'longest-prefix match' mean?
choices: The route that was added to the table most recently wins
When several routes match a destination, the one with the most specific (longest) prefix wins
The route with the largest address range wins
The route with the lowest IP address wins
items.explanation: Longest-prefix match means the most specific matching rule wins, the one whose prefix (the /number) is longest. It is not about which route is newest, has the largest range (that would be the least specific), or the lowest address. Specific beats general, every time.

items.prompt: Which range is more specific (narrower): a /24 or a /16?
choices: /16, because 16 is smaller
/24, because a bigger slash number means more fixed bits and a smaller range
They cover exactly the same number of addresses
Neither; the slash number does not affect size
items.explanation: A larger slash number fixes more leading bits, leaving fewer bits free for hosts, so the range is smaller and more specific. A /24 is narrower than a /16. This is why /24 would win longest-prefix match against /16 for an address both contain.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between a static route and a dynamic route?
choices: Static routes are faster than dynamic routes for every packet
A static route is typed in by hand and stays fixed; a dynamic route is learned automatically by a routing protocol
Static routes only work on IPv6; dynamic routes only work on IPv4
Dynamic routes must be approved by the internet provider
items.explanation: Static routes are entered manually and do not change on their own. Dynamic routes are learned and updated automatically by routers running a routing protocol. Both forward packets the same way once installed; neither is tied to a particular IP version or needs provider approval.

items.prompt: For which situation is static routing the most natural choice?
choices: A tiny, stable network with a single way out to the internet
A giant network with thousands of links that fail and recover constantly
A network where the layout changes every few minutes
A worldwide network connecting many independent companies
items.explanation: Static routes shine when the network is small and rarely changes, so a person can type the few rules once. Large or constantly changing networks need dynamic routing to keep up automatically, and the worldwide case between companies is BGP's job (next lesson).

items.prompt: Why do large networks use dynamic routing protocols instead of typing every route by hand?
choices: Because typing routes is illegal on large networks
Because routers can learn and update routes automatically, and reroute around failures without a person
Because dynamic routes make packets travel faster than light
Because dynamic routing removes the need for any routing table
items.explanation: Dynamic protocols let routers share what they can reach and recompute paths automatically, including routing around a broken link. That is impractical to do by hand at scale. Hand-typing is not illegal, dynamic routing does not change physics, and routers still keep a routing table; the protocol just fills it in.

items.prompt: What is an interior gateway protocol (IGP)?
choices: A protocol that assigns IP addresses to new devices
A routing protocol routers use to share routes inside a single organization or network
A protocol that encrypts all traffic leaving a building
A protocol that connects different companies across the internet
items.explanation: An IGP is the routing protocol used among routers within one organization, so they agree on the best internal paths. OSPF and RIP are examples. Handing out IP addresses is DHCP's job; connecting different companies is the exterior protocol BGP's job (next lesson).

items.prompt: How does OSPF (a link-state protocol) decide on routes?
choices: Each router only asks its direct neighbors how far away things are
Every router shares a description of its own links, so all build the same map and each finds the shortest path
One central computer mails the routes to every router by post
Routers guess and correct themselves only when a packet is lost
items.explanation: OSPF is link-state: routers flood descriptions of their links so every router builds an identical map of the network, then each computes shortest paths over that map. Asking only neighbors how far things are describes the simpler distance-vector style (RIP), not OSPF.

items.prompt: How does RIP (a distance-vector protocol) learn routes, and why is it limited?
choices: It builds a full map like OSPF, so it has no real limits
Each router learns distances only from its direct neighbors, which is simple but slow to react and limited in size
It reads the contents of every packet to decide paths
It requires a satellite link to function
items.explanation: RIP is distance-vector: a router learns how far destinations are only from its immediate neighbors, then picks the fewest-hop path. That is easy to grasp but reacts slowly to changes and does not scale to large networks, which is why RIP is mostly historical. It does not build OSPF's full map or read packet contents.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between link-state (OSPF) and distance-vector (RIP)?
choices: Link-state routers build a shared full map; distance-vector routers learn only neighbors' distances
Distance-vector routers build a full map; link-state routers do not
Link-state protocols cannot be used inside an organization
Distance-vector protocols always find shorter paths than link-state
items.explanation: Link-state (OSPF) means every router has the same complete map and computes its own shortest paths. Distance-vector (RIP) means a router only knows what neighbors tell it about distances. The other options reverse the definitions or are simply untrue; both are interior protocols and link-state generally reacts faster.

lessons.title: BGP, Policy, and the Control/Data Plane
lessons.content:
So far the routers cooperating to find paths have all belonged to a single organization. But the internet is not one network: it is tens of thousands of separate networks run by different companies, schools, and internet providers. Each such independently run network is called an **autonomous system (AS)** and is given its own AS number. This lesson is about the protocol that glues all those separate networks into one internet.

That protocol is **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)**. If interior protocols like OSPF run *inside* one organization, BGP runs *between* organizations. Whenever traffic leaves your provider's network and crosses into another company's network to reach, say, a video site, BGP is what told the routers at that border which way to go. In a real sense, **BGP is the routing protocol that runs the internet** — it is how thousands of separate networks learn how to reach each other.

Here is the part that surprises beginners: **between organizations, the chosen route is often not the shortest one.** Inside one company everybody wants the fastest internal path, so OSPF optimizes for shortest distance. But between companies, the decision is about business and contracts, not distance. A provider would rather send your traffic over a link it gets paid for, or a free link to a partner, than over a link it has to pay another provider to use, even if that path is technically a little longer. This is called **policy routing**: each network applies its own business rules about which neighbor to prefer. That is why the internet needs more than "shortest path" — money and agreements shape the routes.

Because BGP networks largely trust what their neighbors announce, a single mistake can ripple outward. If one network accidentally announces that it can reach addresses it actually cannot, neighbors may believe it and send traffic the wrong way. Such a **route leak** or **hijack** has, several times in real life, knocked large parts of the internet offline for a while. That fragility is the flip side of BGP's openness.

Two last ideas that tie everything together. First, the difference between **routing** and **forwarding**. *Routing* is the slower background work of building the routing table: running OSPF, BGP, and so on to figure out the best paths. *Forwarding* is the fast, per-packet action of looking a packet up in that finished table and sending it to its next hop. Building the table is sometimes called the **control plane**; forwarding packets is the **data plane**. Routers also keep a sense of how good each route is: a **metric** or **cost** compares paths within one protocol (a lower cost is better), and when the same destination is learned from two different protocols, an **administrative distance** says which source to trust more. Finally, after any change, like a link failing, it takes a short time for all routers to update and agree again; that settling time is called **convergence**, and fast convergence is a sign of a healthy network.

**Recap:** the internet is many autonomous systems stitched together by BGP, which chooses routes by business policy, not just distance, and whose trust-based design means one mistake can cause wide outages. Routing builds the table (control plane); forwarding follows it per packet (data plane); metrics and administrative distance pick the best route; convergence is how long it takes everyone to agree after a change.

items.prompt: What is an autonomous system (AS)?
choices: A single router that works without electricity
An independently run network, such as one internet provider or large company, with its own number
A computer that has no operating system
The part of a packet that holds the destination address
items.explanation: An autonomous system is one independently operated network, like an ISP, a cloud provider, or a big enterprise, identified by its own AS number. The internet is tens of thousands of these. It is not a single router, an OS-less computer, or a field in a packet.

items.prompt: What is BGP's job?
choices: To find the best paths between different autonomous systems and glue the internet together
To assign IP addresses to home computers
To find shortest paths inside a single company's network
To encrypt web pages before they are sent
items.explanation: BGP is the protocol that exchanges routes between separate organizations (autonomous systems), letting the whole internet reach itself. Finding shortest paths inside one organization is an interior protocol's job (OSPF/RIP); handing out addresses is DHCP; encrypting pages is TLS.

items.prompt: What is the basic difference between an interior protocol like OSPF and BGP?
choices: OSPF works only on weekends; BGP works all week
OSPF finds paths inside one organization; BGP exchanges routes between different organizations
OSPF is for IPv6 only; BGP is for IPv4 only
OSPF needs the internet; BGP works only offline
items.explanation: OSPF (an interior protocol) optimizes routing within a single organization. BGP (an exterior protocol) connects separate organizations across the internet. The other choices are nonsense; the real split is inside-one-network versus between-networks.

items.prompt: Why does routing between organizations often NOT pick the shortest path?
choices: Because shortest paths are against the law
Because the choice is driven by business and contracts, so a network may prefer a paid or free link over a shorter but costlier one
Because no one has measured the distances
Because shortest paths only exist inside a single company
items.explanation: Between organizations, routing follows policy: money and agreements decide which neighbor to prefer, even if another path is a little shorter. This is why the internet needs policy routing, not just shortest-path routing. Distances are well known; the point is that business value, not raw distance, drives the choice.

items.prompt: Why can a single BGP mistake cause a large internet outage?
choices: Because BGP shuts the whole internet down for safety after any error
Because networks largely trust what neighbors announce, so a wrong announcement can spread and misdirect lots of traffic
Because BGP deletes the routing tables of every router each night
Because BGP can only carry one route at a time
items.explanation: BGP works on trust: if one network announces it can reach addresses it cannot, neighbors may believe and propagate it, sending traffic the wrong way. This route leak or hijack has caused real, wide outages. BGP does not deliberately shut down, wipe tables nightly, or carry only one route.

items.prompt: What is the difference between routing and forwarding?
choices: They are two words for exactly the same action
Routing is building the routing table; forwarding is the per-packet action of using that table to send each packet to its next hop
Routing handles incoming packets; forwarding handles outgoing packets
Forwarding builds the table; routing reads it
items.explanation: Routing is the background work of figuring out the best paths and filling the table (the control plane). Forwarding is the fast, per-packet job of looking each packet up and sending it onward (the data plane). The last option reverses the two; the split is build-the-table versus follow-the-table.

items.prompt: Which pairing is correct?
choices: Control plane = forwarding each packet; data plane = building the table
Control plane = building the table (routing); data plane = forwarding packets
Control plane and data plane both only build the table
Control plane and data plane both only forward packets
items.explanation: The control plane runs the routing protocols that build the table; the data plane forwards packets using that table. Option one reverses them. The two planes do different jobs, so the choices saying they do the same thing are wrong.

items.prompt: What is a route's metric (or cost) used for?
choices: To measure how much the router weighs
To compare paths within a routing protocol so the router can prefer the better (lower-cost) one
To count how many users are online
To set the price a provider charges customers
items.explanation: A metric or cost ranks paths within a single routing protocol; the lower-cost path is preferred. It is an internal measure of path quality, not the device's weight, a user count, or a customer's bill.

items.prompt: The same destination is learned both as a hand-typed static route and from a routing protocol. What decides which one the router uses?
choices: The route with the longest name wins
Administrative distance: it ranks how much the router trusts each source, and the more-trusted source is used
The route that arrived last always wins
The router uses both and sends two copies of every packet
items.explanation: When the same destination comes from two different sources, administrative distance breaks the tie by saying which source to trust more. It is about trust between route sources, not name length or arrival order, and the router installs just one best route, not duplicates.

items.prompt: What is 'convergence' in routing?
choices: The moment two cables are plugged into the same port
The short time it takes, after a change like a link failing, for all routers to update and agree on routes again
The point where two networks merge into one company
The maximum speed a link can reach
items.explanation: Convergence is how long it takes the routers to settle on a consistent, up-to-date set of routes after something changes, such as a link going down. Fast convergence means less disruption. It is not about cabling, company mergers, or link speed.

items.prompt: When your computer wants to reach a server on a different network, how does it decide where to send the packet first?
choices: It runs BGP to compute the whole path itself
If the destination is not on its own local network, it sends the packet to its default gateway (router)
It sends the packet directly to the far-away server with no router involved
It asks the destination server to come get the packet
items.explanation: A host checks whether the destination is on its own local network. If not, it hands the packet to its configured default gateway, the local router, which forwards it onward hop by hop. Ordinary computers do not run BGP, and they cannot reach a far network without going through a router.

lessons.title: Why IPv6 & How to Read an Address
lessons.content:
**Why does IPv6 exist?** Every device on the Internet needs an address, the way every house needs a street address. The older system, IPv4, has only about **4 billion** addresses. That sounded like plenty in the 1980s, but today there are far more phones, laptops, servers, and smart gadgets than that, and **we ran out**. For years a stopgap called NAT (Network Address Translation) let many devices quietly share one public address, but that is a workaround, not a cure.

**IPv6 is the next-generation address system**, built with a huge supply. It's a bit like an old telephone area code filling up, so the phone company switches everyone to a longer number with room for everyone, or a town outgrowing its short ZIP codes and moving to a scheme that can never run out.

An IPv4 address is **32 bits** (a bit is a single 0 or 1). An IPv6 address is **128 bits** — four times as long. Because each extra bit *doubles* the count, 128 bits gives roughly **340 undecillion** addresses (a 3.4 followed by 38 zeros). That is enough to give every device its own public address, essentially forever.

**How is it written?** Writing 128 zeros and ones would be painful, so IPv6 uses **hexadecimal (hex)** — just a compact shorthand for the bits, using the digits 0-9 and the letters a-f. An address is **eight groups of four hex digits, separated by colons**, for example:

`2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001`

Two shortcuts make these shorter. (1) **Drop leading zeros** in each group: `0db8` becomes `db8`, and `0000` becomes `0`. (2) The **`::`** symbol replaces one run of all-zero groups, filling in as many as needed. So the address above shortens to `2001:db8::1`. You may use `::` **only once** in an address, or the length would be ambiguous.

**Recap:** IPv4 ran out of addresses; IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses for an almost unlimited supply, written as eight hex groups with the `::` shortcut for zeros.

items.prompt: Why was IPv6 created in the first place?
choices: IPv4 has only about 4 billion addresses, and the world ran out of them
IPv6 makes web pages load faster than IPv4
IPv4 was found to be insecure and had to be replaced for safety
IPv6 uses less electricity than IPv4
items.explanation: IPv4's main limit is its size: about 4 billion addresses, far fewer than the number of devices in the world today, so the supply was exhausted. IPv6's purpose is a vastly larger address space. Speed, security, and power use are not the reason it was invented.

items.prompt: How many bits is an IPv6 address, compared with an IPv4 address?
choices: 128 bits, versus 32 bits for IPv4
64 bits, versus 32 bits for IPv4
256 bits, versus 128 bits for IPv4
32 bits, the same length as IPv4
items.explanation: An IPv6 address is 128 bits long, four times the 32 bits of an IPv4 address. Because each extra bit doubles the number of possible addresses, those extra bits create an enormous supply rather than just four times as many.

items.prompt: What shortcut does the `::` symbol provide in an IPv6 address?
choices: It replaces one run of all-zero groups with nothing, to shorten the address
It marks the end of the address
It separates the network part from the device part
It doubles the address for backup purposes
items.explanation: `::` stands in for one consecutive run of all-zero 16-bit groups, filling in as many zero groups as are needed to total eight. It is purely a way to write the address more briefly. It does not mark the end, split the address, or duplicate anything.

items.prompt: Why can `::` be used only once in a single IPv6 address?
choices: Using it twice would make it impossible to know how many zero groups each one stands for
Using it twice would make the address too long to send
The colon key can only be pressed once
Routers can only read the first `::` and ignore the rest
items.explanation: `::` means 'enough zero groups to reach eight total'. With two of them, a reader could not tell how many zeros belong to each, so the address length would be ambiguous. That is why the rule allows it only once.

items.prompt: Why does IPv6 write addresses in hexadecimal instead of the dotted decimal of IPv4?
choices: Hex is just a compact way to write the underlying bits, keeping a 128-bit address readable
Hex addresses can hold more information than the bits themselves
Decimal numbers are not allowed on the modern Internet
Hex makes the address encrypted and private
items.explanation: Hexadecimal (digits 0-9 and letters a-f) is simply a shorthand for groups of bits. A 128-bit address would be very long in 0s and 1s, so hex keeps it manageable. It carries exactly the same bits; it does not add information, encrypt, or replace decimal everywhere.

items.prompt: Shorten the address `2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001` using the two shortcuts.
items.explanation: First drop leading zeros: 0db8 becomes db8 and each 0000 becomes 0. Then replace the single run of zero groups with `::`, leaving 2001:db8::1. You must keep 2001 intact (not 21) and may use `::` only once.

items.prompt: What was NAT used for in the IPv4 world, and how does IPv6 change the picture?
choices: NAT let many devices share one public address as a stopgap; IPv6's huge supply means each device can have its own
NAT encrypted traffic; IPv6 makes encryption unnecessary
NAT sped up routing; IPv6 removes the need for routers
NAT translated languages between devices; IPv6 does this automatically
items.explanation: NAT (Network Address Translation) stretched the scarce IPv4 supply by letting a whole home or office share a single public address. It was a workaround for exhaustion. With IPv6's vast supply, every device can simply have its own address, so NAT is no longer required.

items.prompt: How many groups of hex digits are in a full IPv6 address, and what separates them?
choices: Eight groups, separated by colons
Four groups, separated by dots
Six groups, separated by dashes
Sixteen groups, separated by slashes
items.explanation: A full IPv6 address is eight groups of four hex digits each, separated by colons, as in 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Dotted groups of four belong to IPv4; the other options do not match IPv6 notation.

items.prompt: Roughly how large is the IPv6 address space, and why does that matter?
choices: About 340 undecillion addresses, enough to give every device its own for the foreseeable future
About 8 billion addresses, roughly one per person
Exactly double the IPv4 count, about 8 billion
Unlimited, because addresses can be reused instantly
items.explanation: 128 bits gives about 340 undecillion (3.4 with 38 zeros) addresses. That enormous number is the whole point: there is no realistic chance of running out, so every device can hold a unique public address. It is finite, not literally unlimited, but practically inexhaustible.

items.prompt: In the group `0db8`, what do the two shortcuts let you write?
choices: db8, because leading zeros in a group may be dropped
0db, because trailing digits are dropped
0000, because every group becomes zeros
db80, because zeros move to the end
items.explanation: The leading-zero rule lets you drop zeros only at the front of a group, so 0db8 becomes db8. You never drop digits from the end, never move zeros around, and never blank out a group that has real digits.

lessons.title: Address Types, the /64 Subnet & Getting an Address
lessons.content:
Now that you can read an IPv6 address, let's meet the **main types** of address and learn **how a device actually gets one**. Think of address types like different kinds of phone numbers: an internal office extension, a public listed number, and a number that just rings the phone on your own desk.

**Link-local (`fe80::...`)** — Every IPv6 interface **automatically** gives itself one of these the moment it turns on. It works **only on the local link** (the cable or Wi-Fi you're directly attached to) and is never routed to the wider Internet. It's like an internal extension that only works inside one building.

**Global unicast (`2000::/3`, e.g. `2001:db8::...`)** — This is a **public** address, routable across the Internet, like a publicly listed phone number anyone can dial. (The `2001:db8::` range is reserved for documentation and examples, which is why it appears throughout this lesson.)

**Loopback (`::1`)** — This always means **"this same machine."** A program can talk to another program on the same computer using `::1`. It's the IPv6 version of IPv4's `127.0.0.1`.

**The `/64` convention** — The number after the slash says how many leading bits name the **network**; the rest identify the **device** on it. In IPv6 the standard is **`/64`**: the first 64 bits are the network (the *prefix*), and the last 64 bits identify the interface. Nearly every IPv6 network uses `/64`, which keeps things simple and is what automatic addressing expects.

**How does a device get an address?** Two common ways:

- **SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration)** — The device builds its **own** address. A router announces the network's `/64` prefix; the device tacks on its own interface part to form a complete address. No server is needed. ("Stateless" just means no server keeps a record of who got what.)
- **DHCPv6** — A **server** hands out addresses and keeps track of them, similar to how DHCP works in IPv4. Useful when administrators want central control.

Many networks use SLAAC, some use DHCPv6, and some mix the two.

**Recap:** Link-local is the auto, local-only address; global unicast is public; `::1` is loopback. The `/64` prefix splits network from device, and a device gets its address either by building it itself (SLAAC) or by asking a server (DHCPv6).

items.prompt: An address starting with `fe80::` appears on your interface. What kind of address is it?
choices: Link-local: auto-generated and usable only on the local link, not routed
Global unicast: a public, Internet-routable address
Loopback: it always points back to this same machine
Multicast: it delivers to a group of devices at once
items.explanation: `fe80::` is the link-local range. Every interface makes one automatically, and it works only on the directly attached link, never out on the Internet. Public addresses start in the 2000::/3 range; loopback is ::1; multicast starts with ff.

items.prompt: What does the IPv6 loopback address `::1` mean?
choices: This same machine - like 127.0.0.1 in IPv4
The default router for the network
Every device on the local link
A brand-new device with no address yet
items.explanation: `::1` is loopback: traffic to it stays on the same computer, letting programs on one machine talk to each other. It is the direct counterpart of IPv4's 127.0.0.1. It is not a router, a group address, or an 'empty' address.

items.prompt: In the standard IPv6 `/64` convention, what do the first 64 bits represent?
choices: The network (the prefix); the last 64 bits identify the device on it
The device; the last 64 bits identify the network
A checksum used to detect errors
The IPv4 address the device used to have
items.explanation: A /64 means the first 64 bits name the network (the prefix) and the remaining 64 bits identify the interface, or device, on that network. This split is what automatic addressing expects. The bits are not a checksum or an old IPv4 address.

items.prompt: Which best describes a global unicast address such as one starting with `2001:db8:`?
choices: A public address that can be routed across the Internet
A private address that never leaves the local link
The address every device uses to reach itself
A group address for sending to many devices at once
items.explanation: Global unicast addresses (in the 2000::/3 range) are public and routable on the Internet, like a listed phone number. The 2001:db8: block specifically is reserved for documentation. Local-only is link-local, self is loopback, and many-at-once is multicast.

items.prompt: In SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration), how does a device get its address?
choices: The router announces the /64 prefix and the device adds its own part to build the full address
A central server assigns the device a specific address and records it
The device copies the address from a neighboring device
The user must type the full address in by hand
items.explanation: With SLAAC the router advertises the network's /64 prefix, and the device appends its own interface portion to form a complete address, with no server needed. Assigning and recording specific addresses describes DHCPv6, not SLAAC.

items.prompt: How is DHCPv6 different from SLAAC?
choices: DHCPv6 uses a server that hands out and keeps track of addresses; SLAAC has no such server
DHCPv6 works only on IPv4 networks
DHCPv6 makes the device pick a random address with no router involved
DHCPv6 is required on every IPv6 network, while SLAAC is optional
items.explanation: DHCPv6 is the server-based ('stateful') option: a server assigns addresses and keeps a record of them, giving administrators central control. SLAAC needs no server. DHCPv6 is for IPv6, is not always required, and is not server-less.

items.prompt: An interface shows two addresses: `fe80::1a2b:3c4d` and `2001:db8:1::1a2b:3c4d`. What is going on?
choices: fe80:: is the auto link-local address; 2001:db8:: is the routable public address - one interface can hold several
The device is misconfigured; an interface may have only one address
Both are public addresses and the device is on the Internet twice
fe80:: is the loopback and 2001:db8:: is link-local
items.explanation: It is completely normal for one interface to hold several addresses at once: an automatic link-local (fe80::) for the local link plus a public global unicast (2001:db8::) for the Internet. Loopback is ::1, not fe80::.

items.prompt: Which address does an IPv6 interface create for itself automatically, even with no router or server present?
choices: A link-local address (fe80::...)
A global unicast address (2001:...)
A DHCPv6-assigned address
The loopback address for the whole network
items.explanation: Every IPv6 interface generates a link-local (fe80::) address on its own as soon as it comes up, with no router or server. A public global unicast address requires a router's prefix (SLAAC) or a DHCPv6 server, so it is not automatic in the same way.

items.prompt: What does the word 'stateless' mean in 'Stateless Address Autoconfiguration' (SLAAC)?
choices: No server keeps a record of which device got which address
The address has no fixed network prefix
The connection forgets data between packets
The device has no link-local address
items.explanation: 'Stateless' means there is no server tracking the assignments (no stored 'state' about who has which address). The device builds its address from the router's prefix on its own. It does not mean the prefix is missing or that data is forgotten.

lessons.title: NDP Replaces ARP, No Broadcast, and Living Alongside IPv4
lessons.content:
You already met **ARP** in IPv4: to send a frame to a neighbor, a device knows the neighbor's IP address but needs its **hardware (MAC) address**, so ARP asks "who has this IP?" and the owner answers. IPv6 does the **same job with a newer mechanism called NDP**.

**NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) replaces ARP.** When an IPv6 device needs a neighbor's hardware address, it sends a *Neighbor Solicitation* ("who has this address?") and the owner sends back a *Neighbor Advertisement* with its MAC. Same idea as ARP, just renamed and modernized. NDP also handles routers announcing themselves and devices discovering them (which is how SLAAC gets its prefix).

**NDP rides on ICMPv6.** In IPv4, ICMP is the helper protocol behind tools like `ping`. In IPv6, **ICMPv6 does much more**: besides ping, it carries NDP's messages and other essential signaling. This means ICMPv6 is **not optional** — block all of it and core functions like neighbor discovery stop working.

**No broadcast — multicast instead.** IPv4 has a *broadcast* that shouts to **every** device on the link, which bothers machines that don't care. **IPv6 has no broadcast at all.** Instead it uses **multicast**, which delivers only to the small group of devices that have signed up to listen. NDP uses targeted multicast so it disturbs only the relevant device. Same goal as broadcast, but tidier.

**Dual-stack: running IPv4 and IPv6 together.** The Internet can't switch overnight, so most devices run **both** at once — this is called **dual-stack**. The device keeps its IPv4 address *and* its IPv6 address and uses whichever the other side supports. This is the main reason IPv6 adoption has been **gradual** rather than a single switch-over: networks add IPv6 alongside IPv4 and retire IPv4 slowly, with no disruptive "flag day."

**A note on privacy.** Because an IPv6 device can have a stable, unique public address, it could be tracked across websites. To reduce that, devices often use **privacy addresses** — temporary public addresses that change over time — for outgoing connections.

**Recap:** NDP is IPv6's modern replacement for ARP (running on the now-essential ICMPv6); there's no broadcast, only multicast; and dual-stack lets IPv4 and IPv6 run side by side during the slow transition.

items.prompt: In IPv6, what replaces ARP, and what job does it do?
choices: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol), which finds a neighbor's hardware (MAC) address from its IP
DHCPv6, which hands out IP addresses from a server
DNS, which turns names into addresses
NAT, which shares one address among many devices
items.explanation: NDP does ARP's job in IPv6: given a neighbor's IP address, it discovers the matching hardware (MAC) address. DHCPv6 assigns addresses, DNS resolves names, and NAT shares addresses; none of those is ARP's replacement.

items.prompt: How does NDP find a neighbor's hardware address differently from IPv4's ARP?
choices: NDP sends a targeted multicast to a small group instead of broadcasting to everyone
NDP emails the request to a central directory
NDP broadcasts louder than ARP so it reaches farther
NDP asks the user to look the address up manually
items.explanation: ARP broadcasts to every device on the link. NDP instead uses multicast aimed at a small group, so uninvolved devices are not disturbed. The job is the same, but the delivery is tidier. There is no email step or manual lookup involved.

items.prompt: Does IPv6 have a broadcast address, and if not, what does the same job?
choices: No broadcast; IPv6 uses multicast, which reaches only devices that signed up to listen
Yes, IPv6 keeps the same broadcast as IPv4
No broadcast and no replacement; devices simply cannot reach groups
Yes, but only routers are allowed to broadcast
items.explanation: IPv6 drops broadcast entirely. Multicast takes over the 'reach a group' role, delivering only to devices that have joined the relevant group, so uninterested machines are not bothered. Group communication is fully supported, just through multicast.

items.prompt: A network administrator blocks ALL ICMPv6 traffic 'to be safe.' What is the likely result?
choices: Core functions like neighbor discovery (NDP) break, because they ride on ICMPv6
Nothing changes; ICMPv6 is purely for ping and is optional
Only web browsing stops; everything else is fine
The network automatically falls back to IPv4 with no issues
items.explanation: In IPv6, ICMPv6 carries essential signaling, including NDP's neighbor discovery messages, so it is not optional. Blocking all of it breaks core functions. Unlike IPv4's mostly-diagnostic ICMP, ICMPv6 must be allowed (at least selectively).

items.prompt: What does it mean for a device to run 'dual-stack'?
choices: It runs IPv4 and IPv6 at the same time and uses whichever the other side supports
It runs two copies of IPv6 for backup
It stacks two devices physically on top of each other
It switches off IPv4 completely and uses only IPv6
items.explanation: Dual-stack means a device keeps both an IPv4 and an IPv6 address and uses whichever the destination supports. It is not two copies of one protocol, not physical stacking, and not an IPv4 shut-off; the whole point is to support both during the transition.

items.prompt: Why has the switch from IPv4 to IPv6 been gradual rather than a single overnight change?
choices: Networks add IPv6 alongside IPv4 (dual-stack) and retire IPv4 slowly, avoiding a disruptive flag day
IPv6 was only invented last year and needs testing
IPv6 devices cannot be built yet
The two protocols are identical, so there is no rush at all
items.explanation: Because the whole Internet cannot change at once, devices run both protocols together (dual-stack) and phase out IPv4 over time, so nothing breaks suddenly. IPv6 is not brand new and is widely deployed; the gradual pace is about a safe, coordinated transition.

items.prompt: What role does ICMPv6 play in IPv6 compared with ICMP in IPv4?
choices: ICMPv6 does more: besides ping, it carries NDP and other essential signaling, so it is mandatory
ICMPv6 does less and can be removed entirely
ICMPv6 only encrypts traffic
ICMPv6 replaces DNS for name lookups
items.explanation: ICMPv6 takes on a bigger role than IPv4's ICMP: in addition to diagnostics like ping, it carries NDP's neighbor discovery messages, which makes it essential rather than optional. It does not handle encryption or name lookups.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of IPv6 privacy addresses?
choices: They use temporary public addresses that change over time, making a device harder to track across websites
They encrypt every packet the device sends
They hide the device's link-local address from the router
They let two devices share one address like NAT
items.explanation: Because a stable public IPv6 address could be used to track a device, privacy addresses give it temporary public addresses that rotate over time for outgoing connections. They are about reducing tracking, not encryption, hiding link-local addresses, or sharing one address.

items.prompt: In NDP, which message asks 'who has this IPv6 address?' so the sender can learn the neighbor's hardware address?
choices: A Neighbor Solicitation, which the owner answers with a Neighbor Advertisement
A DHCPv6 Request, which a server answers with an address
A DNS query, which a resolver answers with a name
A broadcast ARP request, which IPv6 still uses
items.explanation: NDP uses a Neighbor Solicitation to ask which device owns an address; that device replies with a Neighbor Advertisement carrying its hardware (MAC) address. DHCPv6 and DNS solve different problems, and IPv6 does not use ARP broadcasts at all.

lessons.title: DHCP: How a Device Gets an Address Automatically
lessons.content:
**The everyday picture.** You bring a new phone home, join the Wi-Fi, and it just works. You never typed in an address, a gateway, or anything technical. Behind the scenes, a service called **DHCP** did all of that for you. DHCP stands for **Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol** — a long name for a simple idea: *automatically hand a new device the settings it needs to use the network the moment it joins.*

**Why it exists.** Every device on a network needs an **IP address** (its address on the network, like a house number on a street). It also needs to know a couple of other things, which we will meet in a moment. In the old days a person had to type all of this into each device by hand. That is slow, easy to get wrong, and impossible when guests come and go all day. DHCP removes the chore: a device asks for settings, and a **DHCP server** (usually built into your home router) gives them out.

**An everyday hook.** Think of checking into a hotel. You walk up to the front desk, and the clerk hands you a room key on arrival — you do not pick your own room or rewire the building. When you leave, the room goes back into the pool for the next guest. DHCP works the same way: it hands out an address when a device arrives and takes it back when the device is done.

**What DHCP actually gives you.** When DHCP assigns an address, it usually includes three other useful things, so the device is fully ready to use the internet:

- the **IP address** itself (your number on this network),
- the **default gateway** (the address of the router — the "door" out to the rest of the internet),
- the **DNS server** (the helper that turns names like `example.com` into addresses, covered in a later lesson),
- and a **lease time** (how long you may keep the address before checking back in).

**Recap.** DHCP automatically configures a device the instant it joins a network: it hands out an IP address plus the gateway and DNS settings, so you never have to set them by hand. Next we will watch the short back-and-forth conversation that makes this happen.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does DHCP do?
choices: It automatically gives a device the network settings it needs (like an IP address) when the device joins
It encrypts all the traffic leaving your home network
It speeds up your internet connection by compressing data
It blocks viruses from reaching your computer
items.explanation: DHCP's whole job is automatic setup: when a device joins a network, DHCP hands it an IP address and other settings so it can communicate without anyone typing them by hand. It does not encrypt, compress, or scan for viruses — those are different jobs done by other tools.

items.prompt: What problem did DHCP solve compared with the old way of doing things?
choices: People used to type an address into every device by hand, which was slow and error-prone
Networks used to have no addresses at all
The internet used to be too fast to manage safely
Devices used to share a single keyboard to log in
items.explanation: Before DHCP, someone had to manually configure each device's address and settings. That is tedious and easy to get wrong, and hopeless for devices that come and go. DHCP automates it. Networks always needed addresses; that is exactly the chore DHCP took over.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation is the closest analogy to DHCP?
choices: A hotel front desk handing you a room key when you arrive, and reclaiming it when you leave
A library fining you for an overdue book
A vending machine that only takes exact change
A traffic light cycling through colors on a timer
items.explanation: DHCP assigns an address on arrival and takes it back later for reuse, exactly like a hotel issuing and reclaiming room keys. The other examples do not capture the 'assign on arrival, return to the pool later' idea.

items.prompt: Besides the IP address itself, what else does DHCP commonly hand to a device?
choices: The default gateway (the router's address) and a DNS server to use
A list of every website you are allowed to visit
The Wi-Fi password for the neighbors' network
A backup copy of the device's files
items.explanation: A useful address needs companions: the default gateway (the way out to the rest of the internet) and a DNS server (to look up names). DHCP delivers these together so the device is ready to go. It does not manage allowed-site lists, other networks' passwords, or backups.

items.prompt: The DHCP conversation is often remembered by the word DORA. What do its four steps stand for?
choices: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge
Detect, Open, Receive, Accept
Download, Order, Reply, Approve
Dial, Offer, Reserve, Activate
items.explanation: DORA = Discover (device asks if any server is there), Offer (a server proposes an address), Request (the device says yes to that offer), Acknowledge (the server confirms). The other expansions are made up.

items.prompt: In the DORA exchange, what happens in the first step, Discover?
choices: The new device, which has no address yet, calls out to ask whether any DHCP server is available
The server picks a device at random and assigns it an address
The device downloads a list of all addresses already in use
The router reboots to clear its memory
items.explanation: In Discover, the device has just joined and has no address, so it broadcasts a message essentially asking, 'Is there a DHCP server out there that can set me up?' The server does not initiate; the device speaks first. No address list is downloaded and nothing reboots.

items.prompt: Why must the device's first DHCP messages be 'shouted' to everyone on the local network rather than sent to one specific server?
choices: The device has no address yet and does not know any server's address, so it cannot send a private message to just one
Shouting is always faster than a direct message
DHCP servers refuse to read private messages
It is required so that the neighbors' router can join in
items.explanation: At the very start the device has no IP and does not yet know which machine is the DHCP server, so it cannot address a single recipient. Broadcasting reaches whatever server is listening. Once it has an address it can talk directly. Speed and 'refusing private messages' are not the reason.

items.prompt: DHCP gives out an address as a 'lease' rather than forever. What does a lease mean here?
choices: The device may use the address for a set time, then must check back in to keep it or let it go
The device must pay a fee for the address
The address can never be used by any other device again
The address only works while the device is plugged into power
items.explanation: A lease is a time-limited loan of the address. Before it runs out, the device renews to keep using it; if it leaves, the address eventually returns to the pool for someone else. There is no money involved, and addresses are reused, which is the whole point of a lease.

items.prompt: Roughly halfway through its lease, what does a device normally try to do?
choices: Renew the lease by quietly asking the same server to extend it, before it expires
Immediately give the address back and go offline
Start the whole DORA exchange over from scratch every few seconds
Ask every other device to vote on whether it can keep the address
items.explanation: Devices renew early (around the halfway point) by contacting the server that gave them the address and asking to extend it, so they keep working without interruption. Only if renewal keeps failing as expiry nears does it fall back to asking any server, and finally restart DORA. It does not go offline or hold a vote.

items.prompt: A home printer keeps getting a different address, which makes it hard to find. A DHCP 'reservation' fixes this. What is a reservation?
choices: A rule that always hands the same specific device the same address each time
A way to hide the printer from the rest of the network
A second internet connection just for the printer
A setting that turns off DHCP for the whole house
items.explanation: A reservation tells the DHCP server: 'whenever this particular device asks, give it this same address.' The device still uses DHCP (no manual setup), but its address is now predictable, which is handy for printers and servers. It does not hide the device or add a second connection.

items.prompt: In the Offer step of DORA, what is the DHCP server doing?
choices: Proposing an address (and settings) the device may use, in reply to the Discover
Confirming that the device has finished and is leaving the network
Asking the device for its password
Erasing the device's old configuration
items.explanation: After hearing a Discover, the server replies with an Offer: 'You can have this address, with these settings.' The device then formally Requests it and the server Acknowledges. Confirming a departure, asking for passwords, and erasing config are not part of the Offer.

lessons.title: NAT: Sharing One Public Address Among Many Devices
lessons.content:
**The everyday picture.** Look around a typical home: a laptop, a couple of phones, a TV, maybe a game console — all online at once. Yet your internet provider usually gives your home just **one** public address that the rest of the internet can see. So how do a dozen devices share a single address? The answer is **NAT**, short for **Network Address Translation**.

**First, two kinds of address.** Recall that some IP addresses are **private** — reserved for use inside a home or office and not valid out on the internet. (These come from set-aside ranges, defined in a rule called **RFC 1918**, such as the familiar `192.168.x.x`.) Other addresses are **public** — globally unique and reachable from anywhere. Inside your home, every device gets its own *private* address from DHCP. To the outside world, your whole home appears as one *public* address.

**What NAT does.** Your router sits at the boundary. When a device sends a request out to the internet, the router **rewrites the address** on the way out: it swaps the device's private address for the home's one public address. When the reply comes back, the router rewrites it again so it reaches the right device inside. NAT is simply this translation between *many private addresses inside* and *one public address outside*.

**An everyday hook.** Picture a company with one main phone number and a receptionist. Outside callers all dial the same number; the receptionist routes each call to the right extension inside. The single public phone number is the public IP; the extensions are your private devices; the receptionist is the router doing NAT.

**How replies find the right device: port numbers.** If three devices all visit the same website through one public address, how does the router know which reply goes where? It uses **port numbers** — think of them as extension numbers tacked onto an address. For each outgoing connection the router notes, in a small **mapping table**, which inside device-and-port a given outside port belongs to. When a reply arrives, the router looks it up and sends it to the matching device. (Because the router also juggles port numbers this way, this common form of NAT is sometimes called **PAT**, for Port Address Translation.)

**One catch: connections from outside.** NAT is built around connections that *start inside*. A request from a device inside creates a mapping, so its reply can return. But if someone on the internet tries to start a *brand-new* connection to a device inside your home, there is no mapping yet, and the router does not know where to send it — so it drops it. To deliberately allow this (say, to run a game server or a webcam at home), you add a **port-forwarding** rule that says, 'anything arriving on this port, send it to this inside device.'

**Recap.** NAT lets many devices on private addresses share one public address by rewriting addresses at the router and tracking each connection by port number in a mapping table. Connections that start inside work automatically; letting connections in from outside needs port forwarding.

items.prompt: What problem does NAT solve?
choices: It lets many devices on a home network share a single public internet address
It makes a slow internet connection faster
It stores copies of websites so they load instantly
It assigns each device a brand-new public address from the internet
items.explanation: Homes typically get just one public address, but have many devices. NAT shares that one public address among them all by translating addresses at the router. It is not about speed or caching, and it specifically avoids needing a public address per device — that scarcity is why NAT exists.

items.prompt: How does NAT let one public address serve many devices?
choices: The router rewrites addresses on the way out and back, so traffic uses the one public address but still reaches the right device
It puts all the devices to sleep except one at a time
It gives each device a slice of the day to be online
It copies each device's data onto every other device
items.explanation: NAT works by rewriting (translating) the address fields as packets cross the router: private-to-public going out, and back again returning. All devices can be online at once; they are not taking turns or sleeping. The router's translation is what makes the sharing possible.

items.prompt: Which everyday setup is the best analogy for NAT?
choices: One company phone number with a receptionist who routes each call to the right extension
A one-way street with no turns
A library where every book has a unique barcode
A mailbox that only accepts letters, never packages
items.explanation: The single public number is the public IP, the extensions are the private devices, and the receptionist routing calls is the router doing NAT. The other examples do not capture 'one shared outside identity, many inside recipients.'

items.prompt: When three devices visit the same website at once through one public address, how does the router send each reply back to the correct device?
choices: It uses port numbers and a mapping table to remember which connection belongs to which device
It guesses, and the devices ignore replies that are not theirs
It sends every reply to all three devices
It asks the website which device should get the reply
items.explanation: The router records each connection in a mapping table keyed by port numbers (like extension numbers), so when a reply arrives it knows exactly which inside device to deliver it to. It does not guess, broadcast to everyone, or rely on the website to sort it out.

items.prompt: Addresses like 192.168.1.10 are 'private' addresses (from the RFC 1918 ranges). What does that mean?
choices: They are meant for use inside a local network and are not valid out on the public internet
They are encrypted versions of public addresses
They can only be used by government networks
They are faster than public addresses
items.explanation: Private ranges are set aside for use inside homes and offices; the public internet will not route them. That is exactly why NAT is needed — to translate these private addresses into a public one for traffic leaving the network. They are not encrypted, government-only, or faster.

items.prompt: Why does a normal connection that a device starts from inside the home work automatically through NAT?
choices: Sending the request out creates a mapping in the router, so the reply knows how to get back in
The router opens every port to the internet by default
Outgoing connections skip the router entirely
The internet remembers your private address
items.explanation: When an inside device initiates a connection, the router records a mapping for it; the matching reply is then let back in and translated to the right device. The router does not leave all ports open, traffic does go through the router, and the internet never sees your private address.

items.prompt: You want to run a game server at home that friends on the internet can connect to. By default their connection attempts fail. Why, and what fixes it?
choices: Incoming connections have no mapping yet so the router drops them; a port-forwarding rule tells the router where to send them
Your internet is too slow; upgrading the plan fixes it
The game server must be turned off and on again repeatedly
NAT only works on weekends, so they must wait
items.explanation: A connection starting from outside has no existing mapping, so the router does not know which inside device it is for and drops it. Port forwarding adds a rule pointing incoming traffic on a chosen port to your server. It is not about speed, rebooting, or timing.

items.prompt: This common form of NAT is sometimes called PAT (Port Address Translation). Why mention ports in the name?
choices: Because the router also tracks and rewrites port numbers, which is how it keeps many simultaneous connections separate on one address
Because it only works on physical ports on the back of the router
Because ports are a kind of password
Because each device must be plugged into a numbered port
items.explanation: PAT highlights that the router uses port numbers (extension-like tags) to tell apart the many connections sharing one public address. The 'ports' here are network port numbers, not the physical sockets on the router, and they are not passwords.

items.prompt: Some internet providers run a second layer of NAT on their own side, called Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT). What does that mean for a home user?
choices: Even your 'public' address is shared with other customers, which makes hosting incoming connections from home harder
Your internet becomes completely private and untraceable
You automatically get a faster connection
Your devices stop needing private addresses
items.explanation: With CGNAT the provider shares one public address across many customers (a NAT on top of your home NAT), stretching scarce addresses further. The downside is that running servers reachable from outside gets even harder. It does not make you untraceable, speed things up, or remove the need for private addresses.

items.prompt: How does IPv6 reduce the need for NAT?
choices: IPv6 has so many addresses that each device can have its own public one, so sharing a single address is no longer necessary
IPv6 makes NAT illegal
IPv6 hides addresses so none are needed
IPv6 routers cannot translate addresses
items.explanation: NAT exists mainly because public IPv4 addresses are scarce. IPv6's enormous supply means devices can each have a public address, removing the scarcity that forced address sharing. (Firewalls, not NAT, still handle protection.) IPv6 does not outlaw NAT or eliminate addresses.

items.prompt: From the internet's point of view, what does a home with ten devices behind NAT look like?
choices: A single public address; the ten private addresses inside are hidden from the outside
Ten separate public addresses, one per device
No address at all until a device connects
A list of every website the home has visited
items.explanation: Outside observers see only the one public address the router presents; the private addresses inside are never exposed on the internet. That is the essence of NAT: many hidden inside, one shown outside. There are not ten public addresses, and the home does have an address.

lessons.title: DNS: The Internet's Phone Book (Names to Addresses)
lessons.content:
**The everyday picture.** You type `example.com` into a browser, not a string of numbers. But computers find each other using **IP addresses**, not names. Something has to turn the friendly name into the number the machines need. That something is **DNS**, the **Domain Name System** — the internet's phone book.

**Why it exists.** Names are easy for people to remember; addresses are easy for machines to route to. DNS bridges the two: give it a name, and it returns the matching address. Without it, you would have to memorize numbers for every site you visit — as unworkable as memorizing everyone's phone number instead of looking up their name in your contacts.

**An everyday hook.** Looking up `example.com` is just like looking up a friend's name in your phone's contacts to get their number. You remember the name; the phone book stores the number; the lookup connects the two.

**Nobody stores the whole internet — it is organized in layers.** There are far too many names for one list, so DNS is split into a **hierarchy** that hands questions downward:

- the **root** servers sit at the very top and know where to find each top-level ending,
- the **TLD** servers handle a top-level domain like `.com` or `.org` (TLD = Top-Level Domain) and know who runs each name under it,
- the **authoritative** server for a specific domain holds that domain's actual answers (the real address for `example.com`).

**How a lookup works (the resolution walk).** Your device does not chase all this itself. It asks a helper called a **resolver** (often run by your provider or set by DHCP). The resolver does the legwork, step by step: it asks a **root** server, which points it to the **.com** servers; it asks **.com**, which points it to **example.com's** authoritative server; it asks that server, which finally returns the address. The resolver hands the answer back to your device — and, importantly, **remembers it** for a while so the next lookup is instant.

**Remembering answers: caching and TTL.** That 'remembering' is called **caching**. Each answer comes with a **TTL** (Time To Live) — a countdown saying how long it may be reused before the resolver should ask again. Caching is why the web feels fast, and why a changed address can take a little while to reach everyone (old answers linger until their TTL runs out).

**Different questions, different records.** DNS stores several kinds of entry, called **records**. The everyday ones: **A** gives the IPv4 address for a name; **AAAA** gives the IPv6 address; **CNAME** is an alias that says 'this name is really that name'; **MX** says which mail server handles a domain's email. A few more you will meet: **NS** lists the servers in charge of a domain, and **TXT** holds free-form text (often used to verify ownership).

**A brief word on trust and privacy.** Because DNS is so central, attackers sometimes try **DNS spoofing** — feeding you a fake answer to send you to the wrong place. Two modern protections exist, and you only need to know they are there: **DNSSEC** adds digital signatures so a resolver can check an answer is genuine, and **DoH/DoT** (DNS over HTTPS / over TLS) encrypt your lookups so others cannot read or tamper with them on the way.

**Recap.** DNS translates names people remember into the IP addresses machines need, using a layered system (root, then TLD, then the domain's authoritative server) that a resolver walks through on your behalf and then caches. Record types like A, AAAA, CNAME, and MX answer different kinds of questions.

items.prompt: What does DNS translate?
choices: A name people remember (like example.com) into the IP address machines need
An IP address into the name of the company that owns it
A web page from one language into another
A password into an encrypted form
items.explanation: DNS is the internet's phone book: you give it a name and it returns the matching IP address, because machines route by address, not by name. It is not a translator of human languages or a password tool. (There is a reverse lookup for IP-to-name, but the everyday job is name-to-address.)

items.prompt: Why does DNS exist at all?
choices: Names are easy for people to remember, but machines need numeric addresses, so DNS connects the two
It makes websites load in a different order
It gives every website a password
It stores the full text of every web page
items.explanation: People remember names; computers route to numbers. DNS bridges that gap by mapping names to addresses, so you never have to memorize the numbers. It does not reorder pages, assign passwords, or store page contents.

items.prompt: Which everyday action is the best analogy for a DNS lookup?
choices: Looking up a friend's name in your contacts to get their phone number
Dialing a number at random to see who answers
Memorizing a poem word for word
Sorting your email into folders
items.explanation: You remember a name; the phone book (DNS) returns the number (address). That name-to-number lookup is exactly what DNS does. Random dialing, memorizing, and sorting do not capture the lookup idea.

items.prompt: Walk the lookup: when a resolver looks up www.example.com from a cold start, what is the usual order of who it asks?
choices: A root server, then the .com (TLD) server, then example.com's authoritative server
example.com's server first, then .com, then the root
Only the root server, which knows every address directly
Your own computer's hard drive, which stores all addresses
items.explanation: The resolver works top-down: the root points it to .com, .com points it to example.com's authoritative server, and that server gives the address. The root does not know every address — it only refers you downward — and your hard drive does not store the internet's addresses.

items.prompt: What is the resolver's role in a DNS lookup?
choices: It does the legwork of asking the chain of servers for you, then returns the answer and remembers it
It is the single server that stores every name on the internet
It blocks websites it does not like
It is the same thing as your web browser
items.explanation: Your device asks a resolver, and the resolver walks the hierarchy (root, TLD, authoritative) on your behalf, then caches the result. No single server stores all names — that is why the hierarchy exists. The resolver is not your browser and is not primarily a blocker.

items.prompt: Why is DNS organized as a hierarchy (root, then TLD, then authoritative) instead of one giant list?
choices: There are far too many names for one list, so the work is split and questions are handed downward
Because the internet was built in alphabetical order
To make every lookup take exactly the same time
So that only one company can ever control all names
items.explanation: A single list of every name on earth would be unmanageable and impossible to keep updated. The hierarchy divides responsibility: each level only needs to point one step closer. It is not about alphabetical order, equal timing, or single-company control — in fact it spreads control out.

items.prompt: What does an A record hold?
choices: The IPv4 address for a name
The IPv6 address for a name
An alias pointing one name to another
The mail server for a domain
items.explanation: An A record maps a name to its IPv4 address — the most common everyday record. The IPv6 version is AAAA, an alias is a CNAME, and the mail server is given by MX. Keeping these straight is the core of reading DNS.

items.prompt: What is a CNAME record for?
choices: It makes one name an alias for another name, which is then looked up
It maps a name straight to an IPv4 address
It lists which servers are in charge of a domain
It stores the domain's email settings
items.explanation: A CNAME says 'this name is really that other name' — the resolver then looks up the target. Mapping straight to an IPv4 address is an A record; listing the responsible servers is NS; email is MX. CNAME is purely an alias.

items.prompt: Which record tells the world where to deliver a domain's email?
choices: MX
A
CNAME
TXT
items.explanation: MX (Mail eXchanger) names the mail server that handles a domain's email. A gives an address, CNAME is an alias, and TXT holds free-form text. So MX is the one mail systems look up to find where to send messages.

items.prompt: You change your website's address, but for a while some visitors still reach the old one. What is the most likely reason?
choices: Resolvers cached the old answer and will keep using it until its TTL countdown runs out
DNS permanently refuses to update any record
The visitors' computers are broken
The new address was rejected by the root servers
items.explanation: Cached answers stay in use until their TTL expires, so during that window some users still get the old address. Lowering the TTL ahead of a change shortens this delay. DNS does update; the visitors' machines are fine; and root servers do not approve individual addresses.

items.prompt: What does the TTL (Time To Live) on a DNS answer control?
choices: How long a resolver may reuse the cached answer before asking again
How fast the website's pages load
How many devices may visit the website
How long the domain name is registered for
items.explanation: TTL is a countdown for caching: it tells resolvers how long they may keep reusing an answer before checking for a fresh one. It is not about page speed, visitor limits, or domain registration length.

items.prompt: During the lookup, what does a root server typically return for a .com name?
choices: A pointer to the .com (TLD) servers, not the final address
The final IP address of the website
An error saying the name does not exist
The mail server for the domain
items.explanation: Root servers do not store final answers; they refer the resolver to the right TLD servers (here, .com), which in turn refer it to the authoritative server. Expecting the root to hand back the final address misunderstands the layered design.

items.prompt: At a beginner level, what is the difference between DNSSEC and DoH/DoT?
choices: DNSSEC checks that an answer is genuine; DoH/DoT encrypt the lookup so others cannot read or tamper with it
They are two names for the exact same thing
DNSSEC speeds up lookups; DoH/DoT slows them down on purpose
DNSSEC stores web pages; DoH/DoT stores passwords
items.explanation: DNSSEC is about authenticity (digital signatures prove an answer was not faked), while DoH/DoT is about privacy (encrypting the query in transit). They tackle different problems and can be used together. They are not identical, and neither stores pages or passwords.

lessons.title: Ports, the Right Program, and Reliable vs Fast
lessons.content:
# Getting Data to the Right Program

You already know that IP gets a packet to the right *computer*. But it does best-effort delivery only: packets can be lost, arrive out of order, or never show up, and IP makes no promises. That leaves two everyday problems unsolved. **(1)** A computer runs many programs at once — your web browser, an email app, a chat app. When data arrives, *which program should get it?* **(2)** Do you even need every byte to arrive completely and in order, or would you rather have it fast? The **transport layer** sits on top of IP and solves both.

## Build: apartment numbers and delivery styles

Think of an IP address as the **street address of a building**. It gets the mail to the right building, but the building has many apartments. A **port number** is like the **apartment number**: a 16-bit label (0 to 65535) that says which program inside the computer the data is for. Your browser might use apartment 443; email uses a different one. The pairing of an IP address *and* a port is called a **socket** — it pinpoints one program on one machine, the way "123 Main St, Apartment 443" pinpoints one home. Because every program listens on its own port, the computer can sort incoming data to the correct app. Sorting data to many ports at once is called **multiplexing** (just "sharing one line among many").

With data reaching the right program, you still pick a **delivery style**. **TCP** (Transmission Control Protocol) is reliable and ordered: like a **tracked package with delivery confirmation**, it sets things up first, re-sends anything lost, and hands the data over in the right order. **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) is fast and simple: like **dropping a postcard in the mail**, you just send it — no setup, no guarantee. UDP is great when speed beats perfection (live video, games, quick lookups).

## Recap

The transport layer adds two things over IP: it delivers data to the right **program** using **port numbers** (an IP + port = a socket), and it lets you choose **reliable & ordered (TCP)** versus **fast & simple (UDP)**.

items.prompt: IP already delivers a packet to the correct computer. What extra job does a port number do?
choices: It identifies which program on that computer should receive the data
It encrypts the data so no one else can read it
It picks which computer on the internet to send to
It guarantees the packet will never be lost
items.explanation: A port number is like an apartment number: the IP gets data to the building (the computer), and the port says which program inside (browser, email, chat) it is for. It does not choose the computer (that is the IP's job) and does not encrypt or guarantee delivery.

items.prompt: What is a 'socket' in the sense used here?
choices: A physical plug on the back of the computer
The combination of an IP address and a port number, pinpointing one program
Another name for the entire transport layer
A list of every computer on the local network
items.explanation: A socket is an IP address plus a port number together — like 'street address + apartment number'. That pair pinpoints one specific program on one specific machine. It is not the physical connector, nor the whole layer.

items.prompt: Using the analogy in the lesson, TCP is most like which of these?
choices: A postcard dropped in the mail with no tracking
A tracked package with delivery confirmation
A radio broadcast heard by everyone at once
A phone book listing many addresses
items.explanation: TCP is reliable and ordered: it sets up the delivery, confirms receipt, and re-sends anything lost — like a tracked package with delivery confirmation. The postcard analogy describes UDP, which has no tracking or guarantee.

items.prompt: Which everyday analogy best fits UDP?
choices: A certified letter you must sign for
A dropped-in-the-mailbox postcard: just send it, no guarantee
A locked safe delivered by armored truck
A package you must assemble before it ships
items.explanation: UDP just fires off a packet with no setup and no promise it arrives — like mailing a postcard. The certified-letter and armored-truck images imply confirmation and guarantees, which describe TCP, not UDP.

items.prompt: Why does a single computer need port numbers at all?
choices: Because one computer often runs many programs that each need their own arriving data
Because IP addresses are too short to be unique
Because every packet must be encrypted by the port
Because computers can only send, never receive, without a port
items.explanation: A computer runs many programs at once (browser, email, chat). Ports let arriving data be sorted to the right program. This has nothing to do with IP length or encryption.

items.prompt: What does it mean that IP is 'best-effort'?
choices: IP tries hard and always succeeds in delivering every packet in order
IP forwards packets but does not promise they arrive, arrive in order, or arrive at all
IP only works on the fastest available connection
IP encrypts every packet to keep it safe
items.explanation: Best-effort means IP will forward a packet toward its destination but makes no guarantees — packets can be lost, duplicated, or reordered. Adding reliability on top of that is exactly what TCP does.

items.prompt: Two of your apps (a browser and a music streamer) receive data from the internet at the same time. How does the computer know which data goes to which app?
choices: It guesses based on which app was opened first
Each app uses a different port number, so the transport layer sorts the data to the right one
Both apps share all data and pick out what they need
The IP address alone tells the apps apart
items.explanation: Each running app is reachable at its own port. The transport layer reads the destination port and hands the data to the matching app — this sorting is called multiplexing. The IP address alone only identifies the computer, not the app.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the two new things the transport layer adds on top of IP?
choices: It chooses the route across the internet and assigns IP addresses
It delivers data to the right program (ports) and lets you pick reliable-or-fast delivery
It turns wireless signals into bits and back
It stores web pages so they load faster next time
items.explanation: The transport layer's two additions are process-to-process delivery via ports and a choice of delivery style (reliable/ordered TCP vs fast/simple UDP). Routing and addressing belong to the network layer; signaling is the physical layer; caching is an application concern.

items.prompt: A friend says 'the IP address is enough — we don't need ports.' Why is that wrong?
choices: Ports are needed to encrypt the connection
Without ports, the computer could not tell which of its many programs an arriving packet is meant for
IP addresses change too often to be useful
Ports are what make the internet wireless
items.explanation: An IP address only reaches the computer. Since a computer runs many programs, you still need a port to say which program gets the data. Ports are about addressing the right program, not encryption or wireless.

items.prompt: You are downloading a tax document where every byte must be correct and in order. Which delivery style fits, and why?
choices: UDP, because speed matters more than getting every byte
TCP, because it re-sends lost data and delivers everything in order
UDP, because it guarantees order without any setup
TCP, because it skips setup to be faster
items.explanation: A document must arrive complete and correct, so you want TCP: it confirms receipt, re-sends anything lost, and delivers bytes in order. UDP gives no such guarantee, and it does not guarantee order.

lessons.title: How TCP Works: Setup, Numbering, Re-sending, Teardown
lessons.content:
# TCP, Told as a Story

TCP's promise is simple to state: take IP's unreliable packets and turn them into a stream of data that arrives **complete and in the right order**. To keep that promise, TCP does four things — it sets up a connection, numbers the data, re-sends anything lost, and then closes down cleanly. Let's walk through each like a short story between your computer and a server.

## Build: the four parts

**1. Setup — the 3-way handshake.** Before sending real data, both sides say hello and agree to talk. This takes three short messages. Your computer sends a **SYN** ("synchronize" — *let's start, my counting begins at x*). The server replies **SYN-ACK** (*okay, I got yours, and my counting begins at y*). Your computer sends a final **ACK** ("acknowledge" — *got yours too*). After these three steps the connection is **established** and both sides are ready.

**2. Numbering — sequence and acknowledgment numbers.** TCP gives every chunk of data a **sequence number** so the receiver can put chunks back in order even if they arrive jumbled. Each time the receiver gets data, it sends back an **acknowledgment (ACK)** saying, in effect, "I have everything up to here; send the next part."
