items.explanation: The server issues the ticket with Set-Cookie; the browser then echoes it back using the Cookie header on later requests. Content-Type describes the body and User-Agent identifies the browser -- neither hands out the ticket.

items.prompt: The coat-check analogy compares the SESSION to the coat and the COOKIE to the stub. What does that capture?
choices: The session (your data) stays on the server; the small cookie is what the browser carries to claim it
The cookie holds all your data and the server stores nothing
The session and cookie are the same single thing with two names
The coat and the stub are both kept by the attendant
items.explanation: Like a coat (session) kept by the attendant and a numbered stub (cookie) you carry, the server holds your session data and the browser carries a small cookie to claim it. The cookie is usually just an ID, not all your data, and the session and cookie are two different things working together.

items.prompt: Which protocol turns a name like example.com into an IP address?
choices: DNS (Domain Name System)
SMTP
FTP
MQTT
items.explanation: DNS is the name-to-IP lookup that almost every web action starts with. SMTP sends email, FTP transfers files, and MQTT is for IoT messaging -- none of those resolves names to addresses.

items.prompt: You want to SEND an email. Which protocol does that job, versus reading mail you already have?
choices: SMTP sends mail; IMAP or POP is used to read received mail
IMAP sends mail; SMTP reads it
HTTP sends mail; FTP reads it
POP sends mail; SMTP reads it
items.explanation: SMTP (think 'S for Send') delivers outgoing mail, while IMAP or the older POP retrieves messages you've received. The other pairings reverse or mismatch the roles -- IMAP/POP are for reading, not sending.

items.prompt: You need a secure, encrypted command-line login to another computer. Which protocol?
choices: SSH (Secure Shell)
FTP (plain File Transfer Protocol)
SMTP
DNS
items.explanation: SSH gives an encrypted remote command-line session -- like safely sitting at the far machine's keyboard. Plain FTP isn't encrypted and is for moving files, SMTP is for sending email, and DNS resolves names.

items.prompt: A chat app needs the server to push messages to the user the instant they arrive, with both sides sending anytime. Which fits best?
choices: WebSocket -- an open connection where both sides can send messages anytime
Plain HTTP request/response -- ask once, get one answer, done
FTP -- to transfer files
DNS -- to look up names
items.explanation: A WebSocket keeps the connection open for live, two-way messaging -- ideal for chat and games. Plain HTTP is one-shot (one request, one response), so it can't naturally push messages; FTP and DNS solve entirely different problems.

items.prompt: Tiny battery-powered sensors need a lightweight way to report readings to a central hub that interested apps can subscribe to. Which protocol is designed for this?
choices: MQTT -- a lightweight publish/subscribe protocol for IoT devices
SMTP -- for sending email between servers
HTTP/2 -- a heavier web protocol for browsers
SSH -- for remote command-line login
items.explanation: MQTT uses a lightweight publish/subscribe model built for low-power IoT gadgets: sensors publish, interested apps subscribe. SMTP is for email, HTTP is comparatively heavy for tiny devices, and SSH is for remote logins.

items.prompt: Which statement about application protocols is correct?
choices: HTTP is the most common, but different jobs (email, files, names, IoT) use their own protocols
HTTP is the only application-layer protocol; everything uses it
Email and web pages must both use FTP
DNS replaces the need for any other protocol
items.explanation: HTTP dominates the web but is just one of many application protocols; email, file transfer, name lookup, and IoT each have their own. HTTP is not the only one, email/web don't run over FTP, and DNS only does name-to-IP lookups -- it doesn't replace the others.

lessons.title: APIs, REST/JSON, and Faster HTTP
lessons.content:
## When programs (not people) use the web: APIs

So far we've pictured a *person* browsing pages. But most web traffic is **programs talking to programs**. A weather app on your phone doesn't show you a web page from the weather service -- it quietly asks for the *data* and draws its own screen. The agreed way one program offers its services to another is called an **API** (Application Programming Interface): a defined set of requests a program promises to answer.

## REST: APIs built on plain HTTP

The most common style of web API is **REST**. The idea is comforting because you already know it: a REST API uses **ordinary HTTP requests** to read and change data. Want today's weather for Reno? Your program sends `GET /weather?city=Reno`. The server replies `200 OK` with the data in the body. It's the same request/response and the same methods you learned -- **GET** to read, **POST** to add, **PUT** to replace, **DELETE** to remove -- just aimed at *data* instead of pages.

## JSON: the modern data format

What does that data *look* like? Almost always **JSON** (JavaScript Object Notation) -- a simple, human-readable text format of **labels and values**. A weather reply might be:

```
{ "city": "Reno", "tempF": 71, "sky": "clear" }
```

You can practically *read* that: city is Reno, temperature is 71, sky is clear. JSON caught on because it's easy for humans to read *and* easy for any programming language to parse. When two programs exchange data over the web today, it's usually JSON riding inside HTTP responses.

## Faster versions of HTTP: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3

The original web used **HTTP/1.1**, which mostly handles **one thing at a time** on a connection -- a slow item can hold up everything queued behind it, like a single checkout lane. As web pages grew to dozens of images and files, that got slow.

**HTTP/2** speeds this up with **multiplexing**: many requests and responses travel over **one connection at the same time**, interleaved, so a slow item no longer blocks the rest. Crucially, it's the **same HTTP** -- still `GET`, still `200 OK`, still a body. Only the *delivery* got faster.

**HTTP/3** goes further by running over **QUIC**, a newer transport built on **UDP** instead of TCP. The payoff: if one packet is lost, only its own item waits -- the others keep flowing -- and connections set up faster. Again, the requests and responses are unchanged; it's a faster *pipe*.

The takeaway: **HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 are faster versions of the same idea.** You almost never choose the version by hand -- your browser and the server quietly agree on the fastest both support. (The next sub-topic dives into **QUIC** and these speed-ups in detail.)

## Recap

An **API** lets programs use each other's services; the common web style is **REST**, which is just **plain HTTP requests** aimed at data, usually exchanging **JSON** (readable labels-and-values text). **HTTP/2** (multiplexing over one connection) and **HTTP/3** (over QUIC/UDP) are **faster versions of the same request/response idea** -- the conversation doesn't change, only the speed.

items.prompt: What is an API, in plain terms?
choices: A defined set of requests one program promises to answer, so other programs can use its services
A web page designed for humans to read in a browser
The physical port where a network cable plugs in
A type of cookie used to track logins
items.explanation: An API (Application Programming Interface) is the agreed way one program offers its services to another -- a defined set of requests it will answer. It is meant for programs, not a human-facing page, and it is not hardware or a cookie.

items.prompt: What is a REST API built on?
choices: Ordinary HTTP requests (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) aimed at data
A brand-new protocol unrelated to HTTP
Email delivery over SMTP
Direct database file copying with no protocol
items.explanation: REST is the common web-API style that simply uses ordinary HTTP requests and methods to read and change data. It is not a separate protocol, not email, and not raw file copying -- its whole appeal is reusing the HTTP you already know.

items.prompt: A program sends GET /weather?city=Reno to a REST API. What comes back?
choices: A response with a status code (like 200) and the requested data in the body
Nothing -- REST APIs do not reply
A full styled web page meant for a human to look at
A new IP address for the program to use
items.explanation: It's still HTTP request/response: the server answers with a status code and the data in the body. REST APIs do reply, they return data (typically JSON) rather than a styled page, and they don't hand back IP addresses.

items.prompt: What is JSON?
choices: A simple, human-readable text format of labels and values used to exchange data
A way to encrypt traffic so no one can read it
A programming language used only inside web servers
The header that reports a response's size
items.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, readable text format of labeled values -- easy for humans to read and any language to parse. It is not encryption, not a server-only language, and not a header (Content-Length reports size).

items.prompt: Reading { "city": "Reno", "tempF": 71 } , what does this JSON say?
choices: The city is Reno and the temperature in Fahrenheit is 71
It is an encrypted block that cannot be read
It is an image file's raw bytes
It is a status code meaning success
items.explanation: JSON pairs labels with values, so this literally reads as city = Reno, tempF = 71. That readability is the point of JSON; it is plain text, not encryption, not image bytes, and not a status code.

items.prompt: What problem does HTTP/2's multiplexing solve compared with HTTP/1.1?
choices: It lets many requests/responses share one connection at once, so a slow item no longer blocks the rest
It encrypts the page for the first time
It changes GET and POST into brand-new commands
It removes the need for a server entirely
items.explanation: HTTP/1.1 largely handles one thing at a time, so a slow item stalls the queue; HTTP/2 multiplexes many over one connection so they don't block each other. It does not introduce encryption (that's TLS), does not change the methods, and still needs a server.

items.prompt: HTTP/3 runs over QUIC. What is QUIC built on?
choices: UDP -- a newer transport that lets a lost packet stall only its own item
TCP, exactly like HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2
Email's SMTP protocol
The physical network cable directly, with no transport layer
items.explanation: QUIC is built on UDP, which lets independent items keep flowing when one packet is lost and speeds up connection setup. HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 use TCP; QUIC deliberately does not. It is not built on SMTP and still uses a transport layer.

items.prompt: What is the BEST way to think about HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 versus HTTP/1.1?
choices: Faster versions of the same request/response idea -- the conversation is unchanged, only the speed
Completely different protocols that replace GET, POST, and status codes
Protocols only for email and file transfer, not the web
Older, slower versions that came before HTTP/1.1
items.explanation: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 keep the same requests, methods, and status codes -- they just move the data faster. They don't replace HTTP's commands, they are for the web, and they are newer (faster), not older, than HTTP/1.1.

items.prompt: Who usually decides whether a page loads over HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, or HTTP/3?
choices: The browser and server quietly agree on the fastest version both support
The user must manually pick the version every time
The version is fixed forever and can never change
DNS chooses the HTTP version during name lookup
items.explanation: The client and server negotiate automatically, choosing the fastest version both support -- you almost never set it by hand. It is not a manual per-load choice, it can change as software updates, and DNS only resolves names, not HTTP versions.

items.prompt: A phone weather app shows a custom screen instead of a web page. How does it most likely get its data?
choices: By calling a REST API over HTTP and receiving JSON data it formats itself
By asking the user to read the weather website and type it in
By using SMTP to email itself the forecast
By opening an SSH command-line session to the weather company
items.explanation: Apps typically call a REST API over HTTP and get back JSON, then draw their own interface. They don't make the user copy data manually, email isn't how live app data flows, and SSH is for remote command-line logins, not fetching app data.

lessons.title: The Layered Methodology & the First-Pass Toolkit
lessons.content:
The difference between an engineer who is *trusted* and one who is merely *busy* is methodology. When you get the dreaded "the website is slow/down" report, do not start guessing. Walk the stack, bottom-up, asking one yes/no question per layer and *only* assuming the layer below passed:

1. **Is the link up?** `ip link show` (look for `state UP`), `ethtool eth0` for speed/duplex.
2. **Do I have an IP and a gateway?** `ip addr`, `ip route` (is there a `default via`?).
3. **Can I reach the gateway?** `ping <gw>`, and `ip neigh` to confirm ARP resolved the gateway MAC.
4. **Does DNS resolve?** `dig example.com` — separate name failure from reachability failure.
5. **Can I open a TCP connection?** `nc -vz example.com 443`.
6. **Does the application respond?** `curl -v https://example.com`.

The first step that *fails* localizes the fault to one layer. That is the whole game: you have turned an open-ended "it's broken" into "DNS resolves but TCP won't open on 443" — a sentence you can act on. Top-down works too (start at `curl`, descend on failure); pick whichever gets you to the layer fastest.

```bash
ping -c4 1.1.1.1          # reachability + RTT + loss
ip route get 93.184.216.34 # which gateway/iface will be used
dig +short example.com    # does the name resolve?
nc -vz example.com 443    # can TCP open the port?
```

For someone with a radar/RF background this is the same instinct as isolating a fault to a stage in a signal chain: inject a known good signal, check each stage's output, find the first one that's wrong. Methodology beats tool-trivia every time.

items.front: List the bottom-up layered diagnostic questions for a 'site is down' report.
items.back: Link up? IP/gateway present? Can I reach the gateway? Does DNS resolve? Can TCP open the port? Does the app respond? The first failing step localizes the fault.
items.hint: Six yes/no questions, link to app.

items.front: Which command confirms you have a default route, and what do you look for?
items.back: `ip route` (or `ip route show`). Look for a `default via <gateway> dev <iface>` line; its absence explains why off-subnet destinations are unreachable.

items.front: How do you test 'can I open a TCP connection to port 443?' without an application?
items.back: `nc -vz host 443` (netcat). It performs just the TCP handshake and reports open/refused/timeout — isolating transport reachability from app behavior.

items.front: Produce a layered checklist for a slow/down site (commands per layer).
items.back: L2 link: `ip link` / `ethtool`. L3 addr+route: `ip addr`, `ip route`. Gateway: `ping gw`, `ip neigh`. DNS: `dig name`. Path: `mtr host`. TCP: `nc -vz host 443`. App: `curl -v https://host`. Stop at the first failure; that is your layer.
items.hint: One tool per rung, link to app.

items.front: What does ping's output give you beyond a yes/no answer?
items.back: Round-trip time (latency), jitter (variation in RTT), and packet loss percentage from the summary line — three distinct signals of link/path health.

items.prompt: `ping <gateway>` succeeds but `ping 1.1.1.1` (an external IP) times out. Which layer is implicated?
choices: Local link (cable/NIC) is down
DNS resolution is broken
Routing/gateway forwarding or upstream connectivity beyond the gateway
The application on the remote server is crashed
items.explanation: You reached the gateway (link, local IP, and ARP are fine), so the fault is past it: the gateway's route/forwarding or the upstream path. DNS is not involved because you pinged an IP.

items.prompt: `dig example.com` returns the correct A record, but `curl https://example.com` hangs. The NEXT best single check is:
choices: Re-run dig against a different resolver
`nc -vz example.com 443` to test whether TCP even opens
Reboot the local machine
Check the link with ethtool
items.explanation: DNS already resolved, so the name layer is good. Before blaming the app/TLS, confirm TCP connectivity to the port; nc isolates transport from application behavior.

items.prompt: Why does the methodology say to assume only the layer BELOW the current one has passed?
choices: To make the checklist longer
Because each layer depends on the one beneath it, so the first failure pinpoints the fault without false leads
Because higher layers are always the real problem
Because tools cannot test more than one layer
items.explanation: Layers are dependent: TCP can't open if IP can't route, which can't work if the link is down. Walking up and stopping at the first failure localizes the fault precisely.

items.prompt: Which command both resolves the name AND lets you query a SPECIFIC DNS server to bypass a suspect resolver?
choices: `ping example.com`
`dig @8.8.8.8 example.com`
`nc -vz example.com 53`
`ip neigh`
items.explanation: `dig @<server> <name>` forces the query to a chosen resolver, isolating 'is DNS broken' from 'is THIS resolver broken'. ping would itself fail if your default resolver is broken.

lessons.title: Path, Sockets & Packet Capture (tcpdump + Wireshark)
lessons.content:
Once you've localized the layer, you need tools that *see* what's happening. **Path tools:** `traceroute`/`tracert` map per-hop routers; `mtr` is the one to reach for — it combines traceroute and ping, showing per-hop latency *and* loss continuously, so you can spot the exact hop where loss begins.

**Socket state:** `ss -tlnp` lists listening TCP sockets with owning process (`-t` TCP, `-l` listening, `-n` numeric, `-p` process); `ss -tan` shows all connections and their states (`SYN-SENT` stuck = no SYN-ACK coming back; many `TIME-WAIT` = lots of short connections). `ss` has largely replaced the older `netstat`.

**Packet capture** is ground truth. `tcpdump` uses **BPF filter syntax** — a compact expression language compiled to a kernel filter so you capture only what matters:

```bash
tcpdump -i eth0 host 10.0.0.5
tcpdump -i eth0 tcp port 443
tcpdump -i eth0 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'   # SYNs only
tcpdump -i eth0 'udp port 53'                     # DNS
tcpdump -i eth0 -w cap.pcap 'host 10.0.0.5 and port 443'
```

Filters combine with `and`/`or`/`not`, support `host`/`net`/`port`/`portrange`, direction (`src`/`dst`), and byte-offset matches like `ip[8]<5` (low TTL). Write to a `.pcap` with `-w`, then open it in **Wireshark** for analysis: **display filters** (a different syntax, e.g. `tcp.port==443 && tcp.flags.reset==1`), **Follow Stream** to reassemble a conversation as text, **Expert Info** to auto-flag retransmissions/resets/duplicate ACKs, and the **IO/TCP graphs** and **Statistics** menus to visualize throughput and round-trips. Capture filters limit what you record; display filters limit what you view. Learn both.

items.front: Why prefer `mtr` over a single `traceroute` for intermittent problems?
items.back: mtr runs continuously, combining traceroute + ping to show per-hop latency AND loss over time, so you can see exactly which hop starts dropping packets rather than a one-shot snapshot.

items.front: What does `ss -tlnp` show, flag by flag?
items.back: -t TCP, -l listening sockets only, -n numeric (no name resolution), -p owning process/PID. Together: which processes are listening on which TCP ports.

items.front: Write a tcpdump BPF filter that captures only DNS traffic on eth0.
items.back: `tcpdump -i eth0 udp port 53` (add `or tcp port 53` to catch large/zone-transfer DNS over TCP).
items.hint: DNS is UDP/53 normally.

items.front: Write a tcpdump BPF filter that captures only TCP SYN packets.
items.back: `tcpdump -i eth0 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'`. This masks the flags byte and matches any packet with the SYN bit set (SYN and SYN-ACK).

items.front: Write a tcpdump BPF filter for traffic to/from host 10.0.0.5 on port 443, saved to a pcap.
items.back: `tcpdump -i eth0 -w cap.pcap 'host 10.0.0.5 and port 443'`. `-w` writes raw packets to a file for later Wireshark analysis.

items.front: Distinguish a tcpdump CAPTURE filter from a Wireshark DISPLAY filter.
items.back: Capture filter (BPF, e.g. `tcp port 443`) decides what is recorded by the kernel and cannot be undone. Display filter (Wireshark syntax, e.g. `tcp.port==443`) only changes what is shown from an already-captured set. Different syntaxes.
items.hint: One limits recording, one limits viewing.

items.prompt: Which BPF expression captures only packets WITHOUT the SYN flag to port 80?
choices: `tcp port 80 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0`
`tcp port 80 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn == 0`
`udp port 80 and not syn`
`port 80 or tcp-syn`
items.explanation: Masking the flags byte with tcp-syn and testing == 0 matches packets where the SYN bit is clear, restricted to TCP/80.

items.prompt: A connection shows state SYN-SENT in `ss -tan` and never advances. What does this most directly indicate?
choices: The TLS handshake failed after connecting
No SYN-ACK is coming back: a firewall drop, wrong port, or unreachable host at the transport layer
DNS returned the wrong record
The application returned HTTP 500
items.explanation: SYN-SENT means your host sent SYN and is waiting for SYN-ACK. Stuck there = no response to the SYN, typically a silent firewall drop or dead/listening-less port.

items.prompt: In Wireshark, which feature reassembles a full TCP conversation into readable application bytes?
choices: Expert Info
IO Graph
Follow Stream
Capture Filter
items.explanation: Follow > TCP Stream reassembles the bidirectional byte stream of a single conversation into a readable view (e.g. an HTTP request/response). Expert Info flags anomalies; IO Graph plots rates.

items.prompt: Which Wireshark display filter shows only TCP resets?
choices: `tcp.flags.syn==1`
`tcp.flags.reset==1`
`tcp.analysis.retransmission`
`tcp port 443`
items.explanation: `tcp.flags.reset==1` matches RST packets. Note `tcp port 443` is BPF capture-filter syntax, not a Wireshark display filter (which would be `tcp.port==443`).

items.prompt: `ss` is generally preferred over `netstat` because it:
choices: Captures packets to a file
Reads kernel socket state more efficiently and is the actively maintained successor
Resolves DNS names automatically
Replaces tcpdump for analysis
items.explanation: ss queries the kernel via netlink, is faster on busy hosts, and is the maintained modern tool; netstat is legacy. Neither captures packets.

lessons.title: Reading Captures & Modern Observability
lessons.content:
Capturing is easy; *reading* is the skill. Train your eye for these signatures:

- **Retransmissions / duplicate ACKs:** the sender re-sends because ACKs are missing — points to loss or congestion on the path (cross-check with `mtr`).
- **RST (reset):** an abrupt teardown. A RST right after SYN usually means the port is closed or a firewall is actively rejecting; a mid-stream RST means a peer or middlebox killed the connection.
- **DNS failures:** `SERVFAIL`/`NXDOMAIN` in the response, or a query with no answer (resolver unreachable).
- **TLS alerts:** in the handshake, a `fatal` alert (e.g. `handshake_failure`, `bad_certificate`, `unknown_ca`) tells you the negotiation broke and why — read the ClientHello/ServerHello.
- **MTU/PMTUD issues:** the handshake completes but bulk transfer stalls; you see large packets disappear and `ICMP fragmentation needed` being dropped. Classic on tunnels/VPNs.

The **modern** shift is away from device-centric SNMP polling toward continuous, fine-grained telemetry. Know the categories: **flow telemetry** — NetFlow/IPFIX (per-flow records: who talked to whom, how much) and **sFlow** (packet sampling) for traffic visibility. **Streaming telemetry** — **gNMI** pushes structured (YANG-modeled) device state continuously instead of you polling SNMP OIDs. **Metrics** — **Prometheus exporters** (e.g. node_exporter, SNMP exporter, blackbox_exporter) scrape interface counters and probe results; Grafana graphs them. **Distributed tracing** — **OpenTelemetry** captures a request's journey across services, the application-layer view of network latency. **Kernel-level eBPF** — **Cilium/Hubble** gives identity-aware flow visibility in Kubernetes, and **bpftrace** lets you trace kernel network events ad hoc.

```bash
# bpftrace: count TCP retransmits live by PID
bpftrace -e 'tracepoint:tcp:tcp_retransmit_skb { @[pid] = count(); }'
```

`nmap` (port/host discovery) and aggressive scans are **lab/authorized only** — never point them at networks you don't own. The throughline: methodology localizes, capture proves, and telemetry tells you the fault was coming before the pager went off.

items.front: In a capture, what does a burst of TCP retransmissions and duplicate ACKs indicate?
items.back: Packet loss or congestion on the path: the receiver's ACKs/segments aren't arriving, so the sender retransmits. Correlate the loss to a hop with mtr.

items.front: Differentiate a RST seen right after SYN from a RST seen mid-stream.
items.back: RST after SYN = port closed / firewall actively rejecting the connection. Mid-stream RST = a peer or middlebox abruptly tore down an established connection (crash, timeout, policy).

items.front: A TLS handshake fails. What field in the capture tells you why?
items.back: A TLS Alert record marked 'fatal' with a description such as handshake_failure, bad_certificate, or unknown_ca — read it alongside the ClientHello/ServerHello to see what was rejected.

items.front: What capture signature points to an MTU / PMTUD problem?
items.back: Small packets and the handshake succeed, but large data packets vanish and bulk transfer stalls, often with 'ICMP fragmentation needed' messages being dropped (common on VPNs/tunnels).

items.front: Contrast NetFlow/IPFIX with sFlow.
items.back: NetFlow/IPFIX exports per-flow records (5-tuple, byte/packet counts) summarizing every conversation. sFlow exports statistically SAMPLED packets/counters. Flow = complete accounting; sFlow = scalable sampling for high-rate links.

items.front: What problem do gNMI streaming telemetry and Prometheus exporters solve vs SNMP polling?
items.back: They replace slow, device-centric SNMP polling with continuous push (gNMI, YANG-modeled) or frequent scrapes (Prometheus exporters) of fine-grained metrics, giving higher resolution and faster anomaly detection.

items.prompt: A site loads its login page but every large download stalls and dies. The capture shows the TLS handshake completing, then big packets disappearing. Most likely cause?
choices: DNS is returning NXDOMAIN
An MTU / Path MTU Discovery problem (large packets dropped, ICMP 'frag needed' filtered)
The TCP port is closed
A fatal TLS unknown_ca alert
items.explanation: Small packets and the handshake succeed but large ones fail — the textbook MTU/PMTUD black hole, usually a tunnel/VPN dropping ICMP 'fragmentation needed'.

items.prompt: Which tool gives identity-aware network flow visibility inside a Kubernetes cluster using eBPF?
choices: nmap
Cilium/Hubble
traceroute
netstat
items.explanation: Cilium uses eBPF for datapath and policy; Hubble provides flow-level, identity-aware observability of pod-to-pod traffic. The others are host/path tools with no cluster identity awareness.

items.prompt: OpenTelemetry distributed tracing is best described as:
choices: A packet capture format replacing pcap
A flow-export protocol like IPFIX
Application-layer observability that follows one request's path and latency across services
A kernel BPF program loader
items.explanation: OpenTelemetry traces a request as spans across services, exposing where latency accrues at the application layer — complementary to packet/flow tools lower in the stack.

items.prompt: Why is `nmap` flagged as 'lab/authorized only' in this module?
choices: It only runs on Linux
Active port/host scanning of networks you don't own is intrusive and often illegal/against policy
It cannot detect open ports
It is slower than nc
items.explanation: nmap actively probes hosts; scanning networks without authorization can violate law and policy and trip security alerts. Use it only on systems you own or are explicitly authorized to test.

items.prompt: A `dig` query returns SERVFAIL. What does this most directly mean?
choices: The name does not exist
The resolver tried to answer but failed (e.g. upstream/DNSSEC validation failure), distinct from NXDOMAIN
TCP port 53 is closed
The TLS certificate is invalid
items.explanation: SERVFAIL is a server-side failure (broken upstream, DNSSEC validation failure, misconfiguration), whereas NXDOMAIN means the name authoritatively does not exist. Reading the rcode tells you which.

lessons.title: The Performance Quantities: Bandwidth, Throughput, Latency, Jitter & Loss
lessons.content:
Before you can reason about a network, separate five quantities that beginners blur together.

**Bandwidth** is *capacity* — the maximum bits/second a link can carry, e.g. a 1 Gbps interface. It is a property of the pipe, not of your transfer. **Throughput** is what you actually *achieve* end-to-end, always ≤ bandwidth because of contention, protocol limits, and overhead. **Goodput** is narrower still: the application payload bits delivered per second, stripping out headers, retransmissions, and handshake bytes. A link can run at line-rate throughput while goodput sags under heavy header or retransmit overhead.

**Latency** is delay. Two flavors matter: *one-way delay* and *round-trip time* (RTT), the there-and-back time a ping measures. Most interactive performance is governed by RTT, not bandwidth — a fact that surprises people who think "faster internet" means "more Mbps." Loading a page that needs many round trips is RTT-bound; a bulk download is bandwidth-bound.

**Jitter** is the *variation* in latency packet-to-packet. A VoIP or video call tolerates moderate latency but hates jitter, because the playout buffer must absorb the variance or you get dropouts. This is exactly the timing-stability intuition from radar PRF and clock recovery: a stable interval is worth more than a fast-but-erratic one.

**Loss** is the fraction of packets that never arrive (drops from full queues, bit errors, or congestion). Even tiny loss devastates throughput on long paths because reliable transports interpret loss as a congestion signal and back off. Hold these five apart and most "the network is slow" arguments resolve into a specific, measurable cause.

items.front: Distinguish bandwidth from throughput.
items.back: Bandwidth is the link's maximum capacity (bits/s, a property of the pipe). Throughput is what you actually achieve end-to-end, always ≤ bandwidth due to contention, overhead, and protocol limits.
items.hint: Pipe size vs water actually flowing.

items.front: What is goodput and how does it differ from throughput?
items.back: Goodput is the application payload bits/s delivered, excluding headers, retransmissions, and handshake bytes. Throughput counts all bits on the wire; goodput counts only useful ones.

items.front: Why is much interactive performance RTT-bound rather than bandwidth-bound?
items.back: Work that needs many sequential round trips (DNS, handshakes, dependent requests) is paced by RTT, not Mbps. Adding bandwidth doesn't help if you're waiting on round trips.
items.hint: Think page load with many small dependent requests.

items.front: Define jitter and name a workload that is especially sensitive to it.
items.back: Jitter is the variation in latency from packet to packet. Real-time audio/video (VoIP, conferencing) is sensitive: the playout buffer must absorb the variance or audio drops out.

items.front: Why does even small packet loss hurt throughput badly on long paths?
items.back: Reliable transports treat loss as a congestion signal and reduce their sending rate (back off). On a high-RTT path the recovery and window re-growth take many round trips, so a tiny loss rate caps achievable throughput.

items.prompt: A 1 Gbps link consistently delivers 600 Mbps of HTTP payload during a bulk download. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: Bandwidth is 600 Mbps
Goodput is about 600 Mbps; bandwidth is 1 Gbps
Throughput equals bandwidth here
Latency must be 600 ms
items.explanation: Bandwidth (capacity) is 1 Gbps. The 600 Mbps of delivered payload is goodput, which sits below bandwidth because of overhead, contention, or window limits.

items.prompt: Which quantity does a standard ping primarily measure?
choices: Bandwidth
Goodput
Round-trip time (RTT)
Loss correction overhead
items.explanation: Ping sends an ICMP echo and times the reply, giving round-trip time. It can also report loss, but its headline number is RTT.

items.prompt: A video call has acceptable average latency but stutters badly. The most likely culprit is:
choices: High jitter overwhelming the playout buffer
Excess bandwidth
Goodput exceeding throughput
Too low an RTT
items.explanation: Stutter with acceptable average latency points to jitter — latency variation the playout buffer cannot smooth out.

items.prompt: Which is the best one-line description of the relationship among the metrics?
choices: goodput ≥ throughput ≥ bandwidth
bandwidth ≥ throughput ≥ goodput
latency ≥ bandwidth ≥ jitter
throughput ≥ bandwidth ≥ goodput
items.explanation: Capacity (bandwidth) bounds achieved throughput, which in turn bounds useful payload (goodput): bandwidth ≥ throughput ≥ goodput.

lessons.title: Delay, Queues & the Bandwidth-Delay Product
lessons.content:
Total latency is a *sum of four delays* at every hop. **Processing delay**: the router/host examines headers and makes a forwarding decision (usually tiny). **Queuing delay**: time spent waiting in a buffer behind other packets — the variable, load-dependent term that explodes under congestion. **Serialization (transmission) delay**: time to clock the packet's bits onto the link, $d_{serial} = L / R$ for $L$ bits at rate $R$. **Propagation delay**: time for the signal to travel the distance, $d_{prop} = d / v$, where in fiber $v \approx \tfrac{2}{3}c \approx 2\times10^8\,\text{m/s}$. That two-thirds-of-light figure is your radar/RF timing intuition reused: a packet to a server 3000 km away eats at least $\approx 15\,\text{ms}$ one-way of pure propagation, before any queuing.

**Little's Law** gives queue intuition: $L = \lambda W$ — the average number of items in a system equals arrival rate times average time in system. Rearranged, if a queue holds many packets at a given drain rate, the time-in-queue (latency) is large. You cannot fix a persistently overfull queue by waiting; you must drop or slow arrivals.

The headline tool is the **bandwidth-delay product**: $BDP = BW \times RTT$. It is the amount of data "in flight" filling the pipe end-to-end — the volume of a cylinder whose cross-section is bandwidth and length is RTT. To keep a link *fully utilized*, the sender's in-flight window must be at least the BDP; otherwise it sends a burst, then idles waiting for ACKs. This is why **TCP window scaling** (RFC 7323) exists: the original 16-bit window maxes at 64 KiB, far too small for a "long fat network." On a 1 Gbps link with 80 ms RTT, $BDP = 10^9 \times 0.08 / 8 \approx 10\,\text{MB}$ — you need a ~10 MB window, not 64 KiB, to fill it.

items.front: Name the four components of per-hop delay.
items.back: Processing delay (header inspection/forwarding), queuing delay (waiting in buffer), serialization/transmission delay (clocking bits onto the link), and propagation delay (signal travel time).
items.hint: Two are fixed-ish, one is load-dependent, one depends on distance.

items.front: Give the formulas for serialization and propagation delay.
items.back: Serialization: $d_{serial} = L / R$ (packet bits over link rate). Propagation: $d_{prop} = d / v$, with $v \approx \tfrac{2}{3}c \approx 2\times10^8$ m/s in fiber.

items.front: State Little's Law and its queuing intuition.
items.back: $L = \lambda W$: average number in system = arrival rate × average time in system. A persistently full queue means large time-in-queue (latency); you can't drain it by waiting, you must drop or slow arrivals.

items.front: Define the bandwidth-delay product and what it physically represents.
items.back: $BDP = BW \times RTT$. It is the amount of data 'in flight' that fills the path end-to-end — the pipe's volume. To fully utilize a link the in-flight window must be ≥ BDP.

items.front: Why does TCP need window scaling on a long fat network?
items.back: TCP's base 16-bit receive window maxes at 64 KiB. On high-BDP paths that is far below the BDP, so the sender idles waiting for ACKs. Window scaling (RFC 7323) shifts the window field to allow much larger windows.
items.hint: 16-bit field caps at 65,535 bytes.

items.front: Why is propagation delay irreducible, and how does it tie to radar/RF?
items.back: It is set by distance over signal speed ($\approx 2/3\,c$ in fiber) — no faster path exists. It's the same speed-of-propagation timing that sets radar range resolution and round-trip ranging.

items.prompt: A 1 Gbps link has an 80 ms RTT. Roughly what in-flight window is needed to fully utilize it?
choices: About 64 KiB
About 1 MB
About 10 MB
About 1 GB
items.explanation: $BDP = 10^9\,\text{bits/s} \times 0.08\,\text{s} = 8\times10^7$ bits = $10^7$ bytes ≈ 10 MB. A 64 KiB window would leave the link mostly idle, hence window scaling.

items.prompt: Serialization delay for a 12,000-bit packet on a 100 Mbps link is closest to:
choices: 1.2 ms
12 ms
120 ms

items.prompt: Which delay term is the one that spikes under congestion and causes load-dependent latency?
choices: Propagation delay
Serialization delay
Queuing delay
Processing delay
items.explanation: Queuing delay grows as buffers fill behind other traffic; the other three are roughly fixed for a given packet, distance, and link rate.

items.prompt: A server is 3,000 km away over fiber. The minimum one-way propagation delay is closest to:
choices: 1.5 ms
15 ms
150 ms
items.explanation: $d_{prop} = d/v = 3\times10^6\,\text{m} / (2\times10^8\,\text{m/s}) = 0.015$ s = 15 ms one-way, regardless of bandwidth.

lessons.title: Bufferbloat, AQM, Congestion Control & QUIC
lessons.content:
**Bufferbloat** is the under-appreciated latency killer: vendors oversized buffers so big that a bulk flow fills them and every other packet waits behind a queue that may hold seconds of data. Latency under load balloons even though throughput looks fine — a saturated uplink can push ping from 20 ms to 1000 ms. The fix is **Active Queue Management (AQM)**: instead of a dumb tail-drop FIFO, the queue manages itself. **CoDel** targets *sojourn time* (how long a packet sat in queue) and drops when it stays high, signaling senders to slow before the buffer bloats. **FQ-CoDel** adds fair queuing so a bulk flow can't starve an interactive one; **CAKE** bundles FQ-CoDel with shaping and per-host fairness for home routers. **ECN** (Explicit Congestion Notification) lets a router *mark* a packet (set a bit) instead of dropping it, signaling congestion without a retransmit — a cleaner feedback channel.

**Congestion control** then decides the sending rate. **CUBIC** (Linux default) is *loss-based*: it grows the window along a cubic curve and treats packet loss as the congestion signal, so it tends to fill (and bloat) buffers before backing off. **BBR** is *model-based*: it probes for the bottleneck **bandwidth** and minimum **RTT** and paces to that estimate, keeping queues short. BBR can win throughput on lossy paths but raises fairness debates when sharing links with CUBIC.

**QUIC** (RFC 9000) is the modern transport: a reliable, congestion-controlled, encrypted protocol built *on UDP, in user space*. It folds the transport handshake and **TLS 1.3** into one exchange (**1-RTT** setup, **0-RTT** on resumption), multiplexes independent **streams** so one lost packet doesn't head-of-line-block the others (TCP's flaw under HTTP/2), and supports **connection migration** — a connection ID survives an IP change, so a phone moving from WiFi to cellular keeps its session. User-space implementation lets transport evolve without kernel upgrades. **MPTCP** and **multipath QUIC** extend this to use several paths (WiFi + cellular) at once for aggregation or seamless failover.

items.front: What is bufferbloat and what symptom reveals it?
items.back: Oversized network buffers fill with a bulk flow, so all traffic waits behind seconds of queued data. Symptom: latency under load explodes (e.g. ping jumps from 20 ms to hundreds of ms) while throughput still looks fine.
items.hint: Latency-under-load, not throughput.

items.front: How does CoDel decide to drop, and what does FQ-CoDel add?
items.back: CoDel watches packet sojourn time (time spent in queue) and drops when it stays above target, signaling senders before the buffer bloats. FQ-CoDel adds fair queuing so a bulk flow can't starve interactive flows.

items.front: What does ECN do differently from a normal drop?
items.back: ECN lets a router mark a packet (set a congestion bit) instead of dropping it. The sender learns of congestion and slows down without losing the packet or paying a retransmit.

items.front: Contrast CUBIC and BBR congestion control.
items.back: CUBIC is loss-based: grows the window on a cubic curve and treats loss as the congestion signal, tending to fill buffers. BBR is model-based: estimates bottleneck bandwidth and min RTT and paces to it, keeping queues short.

items.front: What is QUIC, in one sentence, and on what does it run?
items.back: QUIC (RFC 9000) is a reliable, congestion-controlled, TLS 1.3-encrypted transport with multiplexed streams, implemented in user space on top of UDP.

items.front: What does QUIC connection migration solve, and how?
items.back: A QUIC connection is identified by a connection ID, not the IP/port 4-tuple, so it survives an IP change. A phone moving from WiFi to cellular keeps the same session without re-handshaking.

items.prompt: What does QUIC improve over TCP + TLS 1.3 + HTTP/2? (Pick the most complete answer.)
choices: Nothing functional — it's only an encoding change
Combined transport+TLS handshake (1-RTT/0-RTT), per-stream multiplexing without transport head-of-line blocking, and connection migration across IP changes
It removes encryption to go faster
It runs in the kernel for lower latency than TCP
items.explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes streams over one TCP connection, but a single lost segment stalls all streams (transport HOL blocking), and setup needs separate TCP then TLS round trips. QUIC merges the handshake (1-RTT, 0-RTT on resume), gives independent streams so loss affects only its own stream, and migrates connections via connection ID. It runs in user space, not kernel.
items.hint: Think handshake RTTs, HOL blocking, mobility.

items.prompt: Why does a loss-based controller like CUBIC tend to worsen bufferbloat?
choices: It paces strictly to estimated bandwidth
It keeps growing its window until a packet is dropped, which only happens after the buffer is full
It marks packets with ECN instead of dropping
It never retransmits
items.explanation: CUBIC uses loss as its signal, so it must fill the buffer (often a bloated one) before it sees a drop and backs off — maximizing queue occupancy and latency.

items.prompt: HTTP/2 over TCP still suffers head-of-line blocking because:
choices: TLS encrypts the stream identifiers
TCP delivers a single in-order byte stream, so one lost segment stalls every multiplexed stream until it is retransmitted
HPACK header compression serializes requests
UDP reorders packets
items.explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes logical streams over one TCP connection, but TCP guarantees a single in-order byte stream, so a lost segment blocks delivery of all streams behind it. QUIC's per-stream delivery fixes this.

items.prompt: What advantage does implementing QUIC in user space (over UDP) provide?
choices: It bypasses congestion control entirely
Transport logic can evolve and deploy with the application, without waiting for OS kernel upgrades
It guarantees lower propagation delay
It eliminates the need for TLS
items.explanation: Because QUIC lives in user space atop UDP, browsers and servers ship transport improvements with app updates rather than depending on slow, fleet-wide kernel TCP-stack changes.

items.prompt: MPTCP and multipath QUIC primarily enable:
choices: Encrypting the IP header
Using multiple network paths (e.g. WiFi + cellular) at once for aggregation or seamless failover
Removing the need for congestion control
Compressing application payloads
items.explanation: Multipath transports spread or fail over a single logical connection across several paths/interfaces, increasing throughput or resilience — valuable on mobile devices with both WiFi and cellular.

subtopics.title: Wireless Networking: PHY On-Ramp to RF

lessons.title: RF Fundamentals: Spectrum, Wavelength, and the dB Way of Thinking
lessons.content:
Every wireless link starts with the same physics you already know from radar. A signal occupies a **frequency** $f$ and propagates at $c \approx 3\times10^8$ m/s, so its **wavelength** is $\lambda = c/f$. At WiFi's 2.4 GHz, $\lambda \approx 12.5$ cm; at 5 GHz, $\approx 6$ cm; at 28 GHz mmWave, $\approx 1.1$ cm. Wavelength sets antenna size (a quarter-wave whip scales with $\lambda$) and governs how waves diffract, reflect, and penetrate walls — lower frequencies travel and penetrate better, higher frequencies carry more bandwidth but die fast.

Spectrum is either **licensed** (an operator pays a regulator for exclusive use — cellular bands) or **unlicensed ISM** (industrial/scientific/medical bands anyone may share under power/duty rules — 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz, sub-GHz 868/915 MHz). Licensed means clean and predictable; unlicensed means free but crowded, so the protocols must tolerate interference.

RF engineers live in **decibels** because gains and losses multiply, and logarithms turn multiplication into addition. Power ratio in dB is $10\log_{10}(P_1/P_2)$; **dBm** is power referenced to 1 mW, so $0$ dBm $= 1$ mW and $+20$ dBm $= 100$ mW. Handy anchors: $+3$ dB doubles power, $-3$ dB halves it, $+10$ dB is $10\times$. This is exactly the link-budget arithmetic from your radar days — every gain and loss just adds or subtracts on a dB ledger, and you read off the result against a sensitivity threshold. The rest of this subtopic builds on these three pillars: wavelength, band rules, and dB bookkeeping.

items.front: Write the frequency-wavelength relationship and give $\lambda$ at 2.4 GHz.
items.back: $\lambda = c/f$ with $c \approx 3\times10^8$ m/s. At 2.4 GHz, $\lambda \approx 0.125$ m (about 12.5 cm).
items.hint: Divide the speed of light by the frequency.

items.front: Why do lower frequencies propagate and penetrate better than higher ones?
items.back: Longer wavelengths diffract around obstacles and suffer less material/atmospheric absorption, so they travel farther and penetrate walls; higher frequencies offer more bandwidth but attenuate and are blocked far more easily.

items.front: Licensed vs unlicensed (ISM) spectrum: tradeoff in one line.
items.back: Licensed = exclusive, clean, predictable, costs money (cellular). Unlicensed/ISM = free and shared under power/duty limits (WiFi, BLE, LoRa) but must tolerate heavy interference.

items.front: Define dBm and give the values for 1 mW and 100 mW.
items.back: dBm is power relative to 1 mW: $P_{\mathrm{dBm}} = 10\log_{10}(P/1\,\mathrm{mW})$. So 1 mW = 0 dBm and 100 mW = +20 dBm.
items.hint: +10 dB per decade of milliwatts.

items.front: What do +3 dB, -3 dB, and +10 dB do to power?
items.back: +3 dB doubles power, -3 dB halves it, +10 dB multiplies by 10. These anchors let you do link math in your head.

items.prompt: A transmitter outputs +23 dBm. In milliwatts that is approximately:
choices: 50 mW
100 mW
200 mW
230 mW
items.explanation: +20 dBm = 100 mW, and +3 dB doubles it, so +23 dBm ≈ 200 mW.

items.prompt: Which band has the SHORTEST wavelength and thus the most bandwidth but worst penetration?
choices: Sub-GHz 915 MHz
2.4 GHz
5 GHz
28 GHz mmWave
items.explanation: At 28 GHz, λ ≈ 1.1 cm. mmWave offers huge bandwidth but is blocked by walls, foliage, and even rain/hands.

items.prompt: Why do RF engineers express link budgets in decibels?
choices: dB values are always positive and easier to store
Logarithms turn multiplicative gains/losses into additions you can sum on a ledger
Decibels eliminate the need to know transmit power
dB is the SI unit of power
items.explanation: Cascaded gains and path losses multiply linearly; in dB they add, so the whole budget becomes simple addition/subtraction.

items.prompt: 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz, and 868/915 MHz are examples of:
choices: Licensed cellular bands
Unlicensed ISM/shared bands
Satellite-only allocations
Bands reserved for radar
items.explanation: These are unlicensed/shared bands (ISM and similar) used by WiFi, BLE, and LPWAN under regulatory power and duty-cycle limits.

lessons.title: Modulation, Spread Spectrum, OFDM, and the Link Budget
lessons.content:
**Modulation** maps bits onto a carrier by varying amplitude (**ASK**), frequency (**FSK**), phase (**PSK**), or amplitude+phase together (**QAM**). Bits per symbol grows with constellation size: BPSK = 1, QPSK = 2, 16-QAM = 4, 256-QAM = 8, 1024-QAM = 10. Denser constellations pack points closer, so they need higher SNR to keep symbols distinguishable below a target bit-error rate.

**Spread spectrum** deliberately uses more bandwidth than the data needs. **DSSS** multiplies the signal by a fast pseudo-random chip code, spreading energy thin; the receiver de-spreads with the same code, recovering processing gain and rejecting narrowband interferers. **FHSS** rapidly hops the carrier across many channels so any one jammed channel costs only a fraction of the traffic. Both resist interference and hide signals in the noise floor — exactly the LPI/anti-jam logic of military radar.

**OFDM** is the modern workhorse (WiFi, LTE, 5G). It splits the channel into many narrow, orthogonal **subcarriers**, each carrying a low-rate QAM stream. Narrow subcarriers see a flat (non-frequency-selective) channel, which tames multipath and lets a cheap per-subcarrier equalizer work.

The **link budget** is the ledger that decides whether a link closes:
$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - \mathrm{FSPL} - L$$
with everything in dB/dBm. **Free-space path loss** is $\mathrm{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz. The link works when $P_{rx}$ exceeds the receiver **sensitivity**; the difference is your **fade margin**. SNR (or **SINR**, which counts interference too) then sets the highest **MCS** (modulation-and-coding scheme) you can run, and Shannon caps the rate at $C = B\log_2(1+\mathrm{SNR})$.

items.front: What does each modulation family vary: ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM?
items.back: ASK varies amplitude, FSK varies frequency, PSK varies phase, and QAM varies amplitude AND phase together to pack more bits per symbol.
items.hint: Amplitude, frequency, phase, both.

items.front: How many bits per symbol do BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, and 256-QAM carry?
items.back: BPSK = 1, QPSK = 2, 16-QAM = 4, 256-QAM = 8. Bits/symbol = $\log_2(\text{number of constellation points})$.

items.front: How do DSSS and FHSS resist interference?
items.back: DSSS spreads energy with a chip code and de-spreads at the receiver, gaining processing gain against narrowband interferers. FHSS hops the carrier across many channels so any single jammed channel only loses a fraction of traffic.

items.front: Why does OFDM tame multipath?
items.back: It divides the band into many narrow orthogonal subcarriers; each narrow subcarrier sees an approximately flat channel, so frequency-selective multipath fading is handled by a simple per-subcarrier equalizer.

items.front: Write the link-budget and FSPL equations.
items.back: $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - \mathrm{FSPL} - L$ (all dB/dBm). $\mathrm{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ for $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz.
items.hint: Gains add, losses subtract; FSPL grows 20 dB per decade of distance and of frequency.

items.front: Define fade margin and how SNR/SINR maps to rate.
items.back: Fade margin = received power minus receiver sensitivity (the dB reserve before the link drops). Higher SNR/SINR allows a denser MCS; Shannon caps it at $C = B\log_2(1+\mathrm{SNR})$.

items.prompt: LINK-BUDGET CALC: Ptx = +20 dBm, Gtx = Grx = +6 dBi, FSPL = 100 dB, other losses L = 3 dB. What is Prx, and does it close against a -82 dBm sensitivity?
choices: Prx = -71 dBm; closes with 11 dB margin
Prx = -77 dBm; closes with 5 dB margin
Prx = -85 dBm; fails by 3 dB
Prx = -65 dBm; closes with 17 dB margin
items.explanation: Prx = 20 + 6 + 6 - 100 - 3 = -71 dBm. Sensitivity is -82 dBm, so margin = -71 - (-82) = 11 dB. The link closes.

items.prompt: FSPL doubles distance from 1 km to 2 km. How much does free-space path loss increase?
choices: About 3 dB
About 6 dB
About 10 dB
About 20 dB
items.explanation: FSPL uses 20·log10(d); doubling d adds 20·log10(2) ≈ 6 dB. (A decade of distance adds 20 dB.)

items.prompt: Why does 1024-QAM require a higher SNR than QPSK?
choices: It uses more bandwidth
Its constellation points sit closer together, so more SNR is needed to keep them separable below the BER target
It hops frequencies faster
It needs a larger antenna
items.explanation: Higher-order QAM packs 1024 points into the I/Q plane; tighter spacing means noise more easily pushes a symbol into a neighbor, demanding higher SNR.

items.prompt: SINR differs from SNR because it also accounts for:
choices: Thermal noise only
Co-channel and adjacent interference, not just noise
Antenna gain
Cable loss
items.explanation: SINR = signal / (interference + noise). In crowded unlicensed bands, interference often dominates, so SINR is the realistic predictor of usable MCS.

items.prompt: A 20 MHz channel at 20 dB SNR has a Shannon capacity of about:
choices: 20 Mbps
67 Mbps
133 Mbps
200 Mbps
items.explanation: SNR 20 dB = ratio 100. C = 20e6 · log2(1+100) = 20e6 · 6.66 ≈ 133 Mbps. Real rates fall below this from overhead and margin.

lessons.title: Multipath, MIMO, Antennas, WiFi, and Bluetooth/BLE
lessons.content:
Real channels are not free space. Reflections arrive at the receiver with different delays and phases, producing **multipath** that can constructively or destructively combine — **fading**. Slow movement causes deep nulls; this is the radar multipath/clutter problem in disguise. **MIMO** turns multipath from foe to friend: multiple antennas at each end let the system either send independent streams over distinct spatial paths (**spatial multiplexing**, multiplying throughput) or weight antennas to steer energy toward the receiver (**beamforming**, boosting SNR). **Antenna** basics still rule: **gain/directivity** (dBi) concentrates power in a direction, and **polarization** must match or you pay a mismatch loss.

**WiFi (802.11)** runs in 2.4/5/6 GHz with OFDM/OFDMA. The alphabet: a/b/g (legacy), n = WiFi 4 (MIMO), ac = WiFi 5 (5 GHz, MU-MIMO, 256-QAM), ax = WiFi 6/6E (OFDMA, 1024-QAM, 6 GHz), be = WiFi 7 (320 MHz, 4096-QAM, multi-link). Because the medium is shared and a node cannot hear its own collisions on RF, WiFi uses **CSMA/CA** (carrier sense, collision avoidance with random backoff). The **hidden-node problem** — two stations that hear the AP but not each other — is solved with optional **RTS/CTS** handshakes that reserve the channel. **OFDMA** subdivides a channel into resource units so the AP can serve many clients at once; **MU-MIMO** serves several spatial streams simultaneously. Security moved from WPA2 (AES-CCMP, PSK) to **WPA3** (SAE handshake resisting offline dictionary attacks).

**Bluetooth/BLE** also lives at 2.4 GHz using **GFSK** with **adaptive frequency hopping** to dodge WiFi interference. BLE separates **advertising** (broadcast on 3 primary channels for discovery/beacons) from **connections** (data exchange), exposes data through **GATT** (services and characteristics), and supports **BLE mesh** for many-to-many IoT topologies. Low duty cycle is why a coin cell lasts years.

items.front: What is multipath fading and why is it familiar from radar?
items.back: Reflected copies of the signal arrive with different delays/phases and add constructively or destructively, causing fades/nulls. It is the same reflection/clutter physics that produces radar multipath and target scintillation.

items.front: Distinguish spatial multiplexing from beamforming in MIMO.
items.back: Spatial multiplexing sends independent data streams over distinct spatial paths to multiply throughput. Beamforming weights the antenna elements to steer energy toward the receiver, raising SNR/range.

items.front: What do antenna gain (dBi) and polarization affect?
items.back: Gain/directivity concentrates radiated power in a direction (more dBi = narrower, stronger beam). Polarization (vertical/horizontal/circular) must match between ends or a polarization-mismatch loss is incurred.

items.front: Why does WiFi use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD?
items.back: A radio cannot reliably hear its own transmission to detect a collision (no full-duplex sense on the same channel), so WiFi AVOIDS collisions with carrier sense plus random backoff rather than detecting them.

items.front: What is the hidden-node problem and its fix?
items.back: Two stations can hear the AP but not each other, so they transmit simultaneously and collide at the AP. Fix: RTS/CTS handshake that reserves the channel before the data frame.

items.front: BLE: band, modulation, and advertising vs connections.
items.back: 2.4 GHz GFSK with adaptive frequency hopping. Advertising broadcasts on primary channels for discovery/beacons; connections are established for ongoing data exchange via GATT services/characteristics.
items.hint: GFSK + hopping; broadcast then connect.

items.prompt: PER-TECH SUMMARY: Which row correctly describes WiFi 6 (802.11ax)?
choices: Band 2.4/5/6 GHz; OFDMA + 1024-QAM; CSMA/CA access; high rate, short-to-medium range
Band 900 MHz; chirp spread spectrum; ALOHA access; very long range, very low rate
Band 2.4 GHz; GFSK; frequency hopping; short range, low rate
Band mmWave only; SC-FDMA; scheduled access; cellular range
items.explanation: WiFi 6/6E operates across 2.4/5/6 GHz, uses OFDMA and up to 1024-QAM, accesses the medium with CSMA/CA, and trades high throughput for limited indoor range.

items.prompt: What did WPA3 improve over WPA2 for personal networks?
choices: Replaced AES with DES
Introduced the SAE handshake resistant to offline dictionary attacks on captured handshakes
Removed encryption to speed up roaming
Switched the band to 6 GHz
items.explanation: WPA3-Personal uses Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE / Dragonfly), which prevents the offline brute-forcing of a captured WPA2 4-way handshake.

items.prompt: OFDMA in WiFi 6 primarily helps by:
choices: Increasing transmit power
Subdividing a channel into resource units so the AP serves many clients in one transmission, cutting latency under load
Hopping frequencies to avoid jammers
Eliminating the need for antennas
items.explanation: OFDMA allocates subsets of subcarriers (resource units) to different clients simultaneously, improving efficiency and latency for many small flows.

items.prompt: Why can a BLE beacon run for years on a coin cell?
choices: It uses solar power
It transmits brief advertising packets at a very low duty cycle, sleeping most of the time
GFSK consumes no power
It offloads computation to the phone
items.explanation: BLE's low duty cycle (short advertising bursts, long sleep) keeps average current in microamps, so a small cell lasts months to years.

items.prompt: MIMO exploits multipath rather than fighting it by:
choices: Filtering out all reflections
Using the distinct spatial paths as parallel channels to carry independent streams
Lowering the modulation order
Switching to a single antenna
items.explanation: Rich multipath creates decorrelated spatial channels; MIMO spatial multiplexing sends independent streams across them, multiplying capacity.

lessons.title: LoRa/LPWAN (Chirps!) and Cellular 2G to 5G NR
lessons.content:
**LoRa** will feel like home: its PHY is **chirp spread spectrum (CSS)** — the symbol is a linear frequency sweep, the same up/down chirp you used in radar pulse compression. Sweeping across the whole bandwidth gives enormous processing gain, so LoRa decodes signals *below the noise floor*, buying kilometers of range at very low data rate. It uses **sub-GHz ISM** bands (868 MHz EU, 915 MHz US) for better penetration. The **spreading factor** (SF7-SF12) trades rate for range: higher SF means longer, more robust chirps, slower bits, and longer **airtime** — which collides with strict **duty-cycle** limits (e.g. 1% in EU), capping how often a node may transmit. **LoRaWAN** layers a star architecture on top: end devices broadcast to any **gateway** in range (ALOHA-style, no association), gateways forward IP-backhauled packets to a **network server** that de-duplicates and handles keys, then on to application servers. Cellular LPWAN alternatives **NB-IoT** and **LTE-M** trade the unlicensed freedom for licensed-band reliability and carrier management.

**Cellular** evolved 2G (GSM, voice/SMS) -> 3G (UMTS, data) -> 4G/LTE (all-IP, OFDMA down, **SC-FDMA** up to ease the handset's power amplifier) -> **5G NR** (sub-6 GHz plus **mmWave**, massive MIMO, network slicing, private 5G). The architecture is constant: the **UE** (handset/modem) talks over the air to the **RAN** (base station: eNB in LTE, gNB in 5G), which connects to the **core** (EPC / 5GC) for mobility, authentication, and the gateway to the Internet. mmWave delivers gigabit rates but its short wavelength is blocked by buildings, foliage, even a hand, so it needs dense small cells and beamforming. Because operators must address billions of devices and IPv4 is exhausted, cellular networks are heavily **IPv6** (often IPv6-only with NAT64), tying this subtopic back to the addressing modules and forward into topic 07's propagation theory.

items.front: What is LoRa's PHY and why is it radar-adjacent?
items.back: Chirp spread spectrum (CSS): each symbol is a linear frequency sweep (chirp). It is the same chirp waveform used in radar pulse compression, giving high processing gain and decoding below the noise floor.

items.front: How does LoRa spreading factor trade range against rate and airtime?
items.back: Higher SF (SF7→SF12) uses longer chirps: more processing gain and range, but lower bit rate and much longer airtime per packet, which strains duty-cycle limits.
items.hint: Longer chirps = farther but slower.

items.front: Sketch the LoRaWAN architecture end to end.
items.back: End device -> any in-range Gateway (ALOHA broadcast, no association) -> Network Server (de-dup, security, scheduling) over IP backhaul -> Application Server. A star-of-stars topology.

items.front: What are NB-IoT and LTE-M?
items.back: Cellular LPWAN technologies in LICENSED bands managed by carriers: NB-IoT (very narrowband, deep coverage, low rate) and LTE-M (higher rate, mobility/voice) — alternatives to LoRa for IoT.

items.front: Name the three cellular architecture pieces and an example of each in 5G.
items.back: UE (user equipment, the handset/modem), RAN (radio access network, the gNB base station in 5G), and Core (5GC) handling mobility, auth, and the Internet gateway.

items.front: Why does LTE use SC-FDMA on the uplink but OFDMA on the downlink?
items.back: OFDM has a high peak-to-average power ratio, straining the handset's battery-powered amplifier. SC-FDMA has a lower PAPR, so the uplink is more power-efficient; the base station can afford OFDMA on the downlink.

items.front: Why is cellular heavily IPv6?
items.back: Carriers must address billions of devices and IPv4 is exhausted, so mobile cores are commonly IPv6 (frequently IPv6-only with NAT64/DNS64 to reach legacy IPv4 services).

items.prompt: PER-TECH SUMMARY: Which row best describes LoRa?
choices: Sub-GHz ISM; chirp spread spectrum (CSS); ALOHA access; very long range, very low rate, duty-cycle limited
2.4 GHz; OFDM; CSMA/CA; short range, high rate
mmWave; OFDMA; scheduled; gigabit, short range
2.4 GHz; GFSK; frequency hopping; medium range, medium rate
items.explanation: LoRa: sub-GHz unlicensed, CSS modulation, unscheduled ALOHA-style access in LoRaWAN, extreme range at kilobit rates under strict duty-cycle caps.

items.prompt: LINK-BUDGET CALC: a LoRa node has Ptx = +14 dBm, Gtx = +2 dBi, Grx = +6 dBi, path loss + losses = 145 dB. Receiver sensitivity at SF12 is -137 dBm. Does the link close and by how much?
choices: Prx = -123 dBm; closes with 14 dB margin
Prx = -137 dBm; closes with 0 dB margin (marginal)
Prx = -145 dBm; fails by 8 dB
Prx = -110 dBm; closes with 27 dB margin
items.explanation: Prx = 14 + 2 + 6 - 145 = -123 dBm. Margin = -123 - (-137) = 14 dB. LoRa's very low SF12 sensitivity (~-137 dBm) is what enables long range.

items.prompt: A 1% duty-cycle limit on an 868 MHz LoRa device means:
choices: It may use 1% of the available bandwidth
It may transmit only 1% of the time on a sub-band, so high-SF (long-airtime) packets must be sent infrequently
Its power must be 1% of maximum
Only 1% of packets may be acknowledged
items.explanation: Duty cycle caps transmit TIME. Long SF12 airtimes quickly consume the budget, forcing infrequent transmissions — a core LPWAN design constraint.

items.prompt: Why does 5G mmWave need dense small cells and beamforming?
choices: mmWave is licensed and cheap
Its short wavelength suffers severe path loss and is blocked by buildings/foliage/hands, so coverage is short and must be focused
mmWave penetrates walls better than sub-6 GHz
Beamforming reduces data rate
items.explanation: mmWave offers huge bandwidth but tiny range due to high FSPL and blockage; dense cells plus massive-MIMO beamforming concentrate and steer energy to make it usable.

items.prompt: Order the cellular generations by primary leap.
choices: 2G voice/SMS -> 3G mobile data -> 4G all-IP/LTE -> 5G NR mmWave & slicing
2G data -> 3G voice -> 4G mmWave -> 5G SMS
2G all-IP -> 3G slicing -> 4G voice -> 5G data
2G LTE -> 3G NR -> 4G GSM -> 5G UMTS
items.explanation: Roughly: 2G (GSM voice/SMS), 3G (UMTS data), 4G (LTE, all-IP, OFDMA), 5G (NR, sub-6+mmWave, massive MIMO, network slicing).

items.prompt: In LoRaWAN, an end device sends an uplink. What happens at the network server?
choices: It assigns the device a permanent channel
It de-duplicates copies received by multiple gateways, checks security/keys, and forwards to the application server
It performs CSMA/CA backoff for the device
It establishes a GATT connection
items.explanation: Multiple gateways may hear the same broadcast uplink; the network server de-duplicates, validates message integrity/keys, manages ADR, and routes to the app server.

lessons.title: Lightweight TCP/IP Stacks & MCU Networking Hardware
lessons.content:
On a desktop the kernel hides the network stack from you. On a microcontroller with tens of kilobytes of RAM, the stack *is* your problem, and **lwIP** (lightweight IP) is the canonical answer. lwIP is built so you can pick your trade-off. Its **raw / callback API** is the fastest and smallest: you register callbacks the stack invokes when data arrives, with no threads and no copies. The **sequential (netconn) API** wraps that in a thread-friendly message-passing interface, and the **socket API** emulates Berkeley sockets so ported code compiles unchanged — each step up costs RAM and copies. Memory is managed with fixed **pools** (deterministic, no fragmentation) rather than a general heap, and packets live in **pbufs**: chained buffers designed for **zero-copy**, so a payload can sit in DMA-filled memory and never be copied as headers are prepended. Other stacks make different bets: **uIP/Contiki** is even smaller (a single packet buffer, great for sensor motes), **FreeRTOS+TCP** integrates with that RTOS, and **Zephyr**'s net stack is a modern, modular option.

Underneath, an MCU Ethernet port splits into a **MAC** (on-chip, does framing and DMA) and an external **PHY** (the analog/line-coding chip on the magnetics). They connect over **MII** (wide, more pins) or the reduced **RMII** (fewer pins, 50 MHz reference clock) — the same MAC/PHY split you would have probed on radar line cards. The MAC moves frames via **DMA descriptor** rings so the CPU is not copying bytes per packet, and **offload engines** can compute IP/TCP **checksums** in hardware. When a board has no Ethernet MAC at all, you tunnel IP over a serial line with **SLIP** (trivial framing) or **PPP** (adds negotiation and auth).

items.front: Name lwIP's three API styles from smallest/fastest to most portable.
items.back: Raw/callback API (no threads, no copies, smallest) → sequential/netconn API (thread-friendly message passing) → socket API (Berkeley-sockets emulation for porting). Each step up costs more RAM and copies.

items.front: What is a pbuf and why is it designed for zero-copy?
items.back: A pbuf is lwIP's packet buffer, a chainable structure. By chaining buffers and reserving header room, a payload sitting in DMA memory can have headers prepended without copying the data — critical on RAM-starved MCUs.
items.hint: p = packet; chains let headers attach without moving data.

items.front: Why do embedded stacks use fixed memory pools instead of a general heap?
items.back: Pools give deterministic, bounded allocation with no fragmentation — essential when a long-running device cannot tolerate heap fragmentation or unpredictable malloc latency.

items.front: On an MCU Ethernet port, what is the split between MAC and PHY?
items.back: The MAC is on-chip and handles framing, FCS, and DMA to memory; the external PHY does line coding and the analog interface to the magnetics/cable. They connect over MII or RMII.

items.front: What do DMA descriptor rings and checksum offload save the CPU from doing?
items.back: Descriptor rings let the MAC move frames to/from RAM without the CPU copying bytes per packet; checksum/TCP offload computes IP/TCP checksums in hardware instead of the CPU walking every byte.

items.front: When an MCU has no Ethernet MAC, how do you still run IP? SLIP vs PPP?
items.back: Tunnel IP over a serial UART. SLIP is minimal framing (just byte stuffing, no negotiation). PPP adds link negotiation (LCP), authentication, and multi-protocol support.

items.prompt: A team ports a Linux socket application to an STM32 running lwIP with minimal code changes. Which lwIP API did they most likely use, and what is the cost?
choices: Raw/callback API; it copies every packet twice
Socket API; it costs more RAM and data copies than the raw API
DMA API; it bypasses the stack entirely
PPP API; it requires a second MCU
items.explanation: The socket API emulates Berkeley sockets so existing code compiles, but the abstraction and threading cost more RAM and copies than the raw/callback path.

items.prompt: RMII is chosen over MII on a pin-constrained MCU primarily because RMII:
choices: Encrypts the MAC-to-PHY link
Uses fewer signal pins by running a shared 50 MHz reference clock
Eliminates the need for an external PHY
Doubles the line rate to 200 Mbps
items.explanation: RMII (Reduced MII) cuts the pin count of the MAC–PHY interface by using a single 50 MHz reference clock and narrower data buses; it does not remove the PHY or change the wire rate.

items.prompt: Which stack is the best match for an extremely RAM-limited sensor mote that handles one packet at a time?
choices: lwIP socket API with full TCP
FreeRTOS+TCP with multiple sockets
uIP/Contiki with a single shared packet buffer
A desktop Linux kernel stack
items.explanation: uIP/Contiki is designed for the smallest devices, using a single global packet buffer and processing one packet at a time to fit in kilobytes of RAM.

items.prompt: Why are fixed pbuf pools preferable to malloc/free on a device meant to run for months unattended?
choices: malloc is illegal in C on microcontrollers
Pools guarantee bounded, fragmentation-free allocation and predictable latency
Pools automatically encrypt packet contents
malloc cannot allocate buffers larger than 64 bytes
items.explanation: Long-running embedded systems cannot tolerate heap fragmentation or unpredictable allocation latency; fixed pools give deterministic, bounded behavior.

lessons.title: Constrained Protocols: MQTT-SN, CoAP/DTLS, 6LoWPAN & Thread/Matter
lessons.content:
Once IP reaches a tiny device, the *application* protocols also have to shrink. Plain MQTT runs over TCP, which is heavy for the smallest radios, so **MQTT-SN** (for Sensor Networks) maps the same publish/subscribe model onto **UDP** (or even non-IP links), shortens topic names to two-byte IDs, and adds gateways and sleep-aware semantics. **CoAP** takes a different route: a compact, binary, RESTful protocol (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE with response codes that mirror HTTP) running over **UDP**, secured by **DTLS** (TLS adapted for datagrams). The reason **pub/sub and UDP fit lossy, constrained links** is structural: UDP has no handshake, no connection state to keep alive, and no head-of-line blocking, so a device can wake, send one datagram, and sleep — while the broker decouples senders from receivers so a sleeping node misses nothing it subscribes to. Reliability, when needed, is added lightly (CoAP confirmable messages, MQTT QoS) rather than baked into a stream.

At the link layer, **802.15.4** radios carry only ~127-byte frames, far too small for a 40-byte IPv6 header. **6LoWPAN** is the adaptation layer that makes IPv6 fit: it **compresses** the IPv6/UDP headers (eliding fields derivable from link context, e.g. deriving the interface ID from the L2 address) and fragments larger packets. On top of 6LoWPAN sits **Thread**, a self-healing IPv6 mesh for the home, and **Matter**, the application/data-model layer that runs over Thread (and WiFi) to unify smart-home devices across vendors — the modern convergence point. Net effect: a coin-cell sensor speaks real IPv6 end-to-end, just heavily squeezed.

items.front: How does MQTT-SN differ from MQTT for constrained devices?
items.back: MQTT-SN runs the same pub/sub model over UDP (or non-IP links) instead of TCP, uses short 2-byte topic IDs instead of strings, adds gateways, and includes sleep-aware semantics for battery nodes.

items.front: What is CoAP, what transport does it use, and how is it secured?
items.back: CoAP is a compact binary RESTful protocol (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE with HTTP-like response codes) for constrained devices. It runs over UDP and is secured with DTLS (TLS adapted for datagrams).

items.front: Why do pub/sub plus UDP fit lossy, low-power links so well?
items.back: UDP has no handshake, no kept-alive connection state, and no head-of-line blocking, so a node can wake, fire one datagram, and sleep. Pub/sub decouples senders from receivers via a broker, so a sleeping subscriber misses nothing.
items.hint: No connection to babysit; broker buffers the decoupling.

items.front: What problem does 6LoWPAN solve, and how?
items.back: 802.15.4 frames are only ~127 bytes — too small for a 40-byte IPv6 header. 6LoWPAN is an adaptation layer that compresses IPv6/UDP headers (eliding context-derivable fields) and fragments larger packets so IPv6 fits.

items.front: How does header compression derive the IPv6 interface identifier on 802.15.4?
items.back: It can be elided and reconstructed from the link-layer (L2) address, since the interface ID is derivable from context — so those bytes need not travel in the compressed header.

items.front: Distinguish Thread from Matter.
items.back: Thread is a self-healing low-power IPv6 mesh network (built on 802.15.4/6LoWPAN). Matter is the application/data-model layer that runs over Thread or WiFi to make smart-home devices interoperate across vendors.

items.prompt: A battery sensor on an 802.15.4 mesh must report once a minute and sleep otherwise. Why is CoAP-over-UDP a better fit than HTTP-over-TCP?
choices: TCP cannot carry sensor data
UDP avoids a connection handshake and kept-alive state, so the node can fire one datagram and sleep
HTTP cannot be secured at all
CoAP guarantees delivery without any acknowledgements
items.explanation: TCP's handshake and connection keep-alive cost energy and RAM for a node that mostly sleeps. UDP is stateless; CoAP adds optional confirmable reliability only when needed.

items.prompt: DTLS exists rather than plain TLS for CoAP because:
choices: DTLS uses stronger ciphers than TLS
TLS assumes a reliable, ordered byte stream; DTLS adapts the handshake/record layer to unreliable UDP datagrams
DTLS is unencrypted but authenticated
CoAP devices cannot do public-key cryptography
items.explanation: TLS depends on TCP's reliable ordered stream. DTLS modifies the record and handshake layers to tolerate loss, reordering, and the datagram model of UDP.

items.prompt: An IPv6 packet must cross an 802.15.4 link with a ~127-byte MTU. What does 6LoWPAN do?
choices: Drops the packet because IPv6 is incompatible with 802.15.4
Compresses the IPv6/UDP headers and fragments the packet to fit the small frame
Converts the packet to IPv4 to save space
Disables addressing to make room for payload
items.explanation: 6LoWPAN is the adaptation layer that header-compresses and fragments IPv6 so it physically fits the tiny 802.15.4 frame while remaining real IPv6 end-to-end.

items.prompt: In the modern smart-home stack, what is Matter's role relative to Thread?
choices: Matter is the radio PHY; Thread is the cipher suite
Matter is the cross-vendor application/data-model layer; Thread is the underlying low-power IPv6 mesh transport
They are competing radios and cannot coexist
Thread replaces IPv6 entirely with a proprietary scheme
items.explanation: Thread provides the IPv6 mesh network; Matter sits above it (and WiFi) as the interoperable application/data model so devices from different vendors work together.

lessons.title: Edge Security, Power-vs-Connectivity & the End-to-End Trace
lessons.content:
A networked sensor is only useful if it is **trustworthy** and **lives on its battery**, and those two goals fight each other. Security on a constrained device starts at boot: **secure boot** verifies a cryptographic signature on the firmware before running it, so an attacker cannot swap in malicious code; a hardware **root of trust** anchors that chain. Link confidentiality comes from **DTLS** (for UDP/CoAP) or TLS (for TCP/MQTT), but public-key math is expensive, so devices lean on **hardware crypto** — AES/SHA accelerators and a secure element holding keys that never leave the chip. The pattern is: do the costly key exchange rarely, then ride a cheap symmetric session.

The **power-vs-connectivity** trade-off dominates everything else. A radio that is always listening drains a coin cell in days, so devices **duty-cycle**: the MCU and radio sleep most of the time and wake on a timer to sample and transmit. To stay reachable while asleep, designs use **wake-on-radio** — a low-power receiver that listens for a wake-up pattern and only then powers the main radio — or they accept latency and poll.
